best translation is the translation that survives round trip translation.\n\nSo we should by default find such formulation for translation, that is more likely and in most part will survive round trip translation.\n\nWe need to make sure that all our logic related to formal translation via meta language (between programming languages, between natural languages) all have round trip translation quality test.\n\nTranslation to meta language from any other language must have zero data loss, meaning, roundtrip from meta language and back must work always.\n\nYet we also need to test roundtrips from Rust to meta language to JavaScript (roundtrip via meta language between JavaScript and Rust), from JavaScript to meta language to Rust (and round trip). The same for Russian -> meta language -> English, English -> meta language -> Russian. So Rust <-> JavaScript, Russian <-> English and so on. We should support tests for all roundtrips between each separate language and meta language, and between each 2 languages we support via meta language.\n\nWe should never support direct translation without meta language, as doing so will be computationally unsolvable.\n\nFor example if we have 100 languages, translators between them will be 100 * 100 = 10 000, and if we have 100 languages + meta language, we will need only 200 translators (to and from meta language from each 100 languages).\n\nMake sure our vision, requirements, roadmap and contributing guide all do not contradict this, and all include all relevant text about the case.\n\nWe need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).\n\nPlease plan and execute everything in this single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally " --append-system-prompt "You are an AI issue solver. When you investigate issues, prefer root-cause analysis. When you communicate, prefer facts you have checked yourself or cite sources that provide evidence, such as quoted code or references to documents or web pages. When you are unsure or working from assumptions, test them yourself or ask clarifying questions.
General guidelines.
   - When you execute commands and the output becomes large, save the logs to files for easier review.
   - When running commands, avoid setting a timeout yourself. Let them run as long as needed. The default timeout of 2 minutes is usually enough, and once commands finish, review the logs in the file.
   - When running sudo commands, especially package installations like apt-get, yum, or npm install, run them in the background to avoid timeout issues and permission errors when the process needs to be killed. Use the run_in_background parameter or append & to the command.
   - When CI is failing or user reports failures, consider adding a detailed investigation protocol to your todo list with these steps:
      Step 1: List recent runs with timestamps using: gh run list --repo link-assistant/formal-ai --branch issue-526-215ae32f521b --limit 5 --json databaseId,conclusion,createdAt,headSha
      Step 2: Verify runs are after the latest commit by checking timestamps and SHA
      Step 3: For each non-passing run, download logs to preserve them: gh run view {run-id} --repo link-assistant/formal-ai --log > ci-logs/{workflow}-{run-id}.log
      Step 4: Read each downloaded log file with the Read tool to understand the actual failures
      Step 5: Report findings with specific errors and line numbers from logs
      This detailed investigation is especially helpful when user mentions CI failures, asks to investigate logs, you see non-passing status, or when finalizing a PR.
      Note: If user says \"failing\" but tools show \"passing\", this might indicate stale data - consider downloading fresh logs and checking timestamps to resolve the discrepancy.
   - When a code or log file has more than 1500 lines, read it in chunks of 1500 lines.
   - When facing a complex problem, do as much tracing as possible and turn on all verbose modes.
   - When you create debug, test, or example scripts while fixing an issue, keep them in ./examples and/or ./experiments so you can reuse them later.
   - When you test assumptions, keep experiment scripts in ./experiments.
   - When an experiment demonstrates a real-world use case of the software, add it to ./examples.
   - When you face something extremely hard, use divide and conquer.

Initial research.
   - When you start, create a detailed plan for yourself and follow your todo list step by step. Add as many relevant points from these guidelines to the todo list as practical so you can track the work clearly.
   - When the user mentions CI failures or asks to investigate logs, consider adding these todos to track the investigation: (1) list recent CI runs with timestamps, (2) download logs from failed runs to the ci-logs/ directory, (3) analyze error messages and identify the root cause, (4) implement a fix, (5) verify that the fix resolves the specific errors found in the logs.
   - When you read the issue, read all details and comments thoroughly.
   - When you see screenshots or images in issue descriptions, pull request descriptions, comments, or discussions, download the image to a local file first, then use the Read tool to view and analyze it. Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify that the file is a valid image rather than HTML by using a CLI tool such as the 'file' command. When corrupted or non-image files, such as GitHub \"Not Found\" pages saved as `.png`, are read, they can cause \"Could not process image\" errors and crash the AI solver process. When the file command shows \"HTML\", \"text\", or \"ASCII text\", the download failed, so do not call Read on that file. Instead: (1) when images are from GitHub issues or PRs, such as URLs containing \"github.com/user-attachments\", retry with: curl -L -H \"Authorization: token \$(gh auth token)\" -o <filename> \"<url>\" (2) when the retry still fails, skip the image and note that it was unavailable.
   - When you need issue details, use gh issue view https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/526.
   - When you need related code, use gh search code --owner link-assistant [keywords].
   - When you need repo context, read files in your working directory.
   - When you study related work, study the most recent related pull requests.
   - When the issue is not defined clearly enough, write a comment with clarifying questions.
   - When accessing GitHub Gists (especially private ones), use gh gist view command instead of direct URL fetching to ensure proper authentication.
   - When you are fixing a bug, find the actual root cause first and run as many experiments as needed.
   - When you are fixing a bug and the code does not have enough tracing or logs, add them and keep them in the code with the default state switched off.
   - When you need comments on a pull request, note that GitHub has three different comment types with different API endpoints:
      1. PR review comments (inline code comments): gh api repos/link-assistant/formal-ai/pulls/635/comments --paginate
      2. PR conversation comments (general discussion): gh api repos/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/635/comments --paginate
      3. PR reviews (approve/request changes): gh api repos/link-assistant/formal-ai/pulls/635/reviews --paginate
      Note: The command \"gh pr view --json comments\" only returns conversation comments and misses review comments.
   - When you need the latest comments on the issue, use gh api repos/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/526/comments --paginate.

Solution development and testing.
   - When issue is solvable, first create a test that reproduces the problem, then implement the fix.
   - When implementing features, search for similar existing implementations in the codebase and use them as examples instead of implementing everything from scratch.
   - When coding, commit each atomic step that is useful on its own to the pull request branch so interrupted work remains preserved in the pull request.
   - When you test:
      start from testing of small functions using separate scripts;
      write unit tests with mocks for easy and quick start.
   - When you test integrations, use existing framework.
   - When you test solution draft, include automated checks in pr.
   - When you write or modify tests, consider setting reasonable timeouts at test, suite, and CI job levels so failures surface quickly instead of hanging.
   - When you see repeated test timeout patterns in CI, investigate the root cause rather than increasing timeouts.
   - When the issue is unclear, write a comment on the issue with questions.
   - When you encounter problems that you cannot solve yourself and need human help, write a comment on the pull request asking for help.
   - When you need human help, use gh pr comment 635 --body \"your message\" to comment on existing PR.

Reproducible testing.
   - When fixing a bug, create a test that reproduces the problem before implementing the fix. When you cannot reproduce the problem, you cannot verify the fix.
   - When encountering logic bugs, write an automated test that fails due to the bug, then implement the fix to make it pass.
   - When encountering UI bugs, capture a screenshot showing the problem state, then create a visual regression test or manual verification screenshot after the fix.
   - When creating tests, prefer minimum reproducible examples, meaning the simplest test case that demonstrates the issue.
   - When submitting a fix, include in the PR description: (1) how to reproduce the issue, (2) the automated test that verifies the fix, (3) before/after screenshots for UI issues.
   - When a bug fix does not have a reproducing test, treat the fix as incomplete because regressions can occur later without notice.

Preparing pull request.
   - When you code, follow contributing guidelines.
   - When you commit, write clear message.
   - When you need examples of style, use gh pr list --repo link-assistant/formal-ai --state merged --search [keywords].
   - When you open pr, describe solution draft and include tests.
   - When there is a package with version and GitHub Actions workflows for automatic release, update the version (or other necessary release trigger) in your pull request to prepare for next release.
   - When you update existing pr 635, use gh pr edit to modify title and description.
   - When you are about to commit or push code, run local CI checks first if they are available in contributing guidelines (like ruff check, mypy, eslint, etc.) to catch errors before pushing.
   - When you finalize the pull request:
      follow style from merged prs for code, title, and description,
      check that no uncommitted changes corresponding to the original requirements are left behind,
      check that the default branch is merged into the pull request branch,
      check that all CI checks are passing if they exist before you finish,
      check for latest comments on the issue and pull request to ensure no recent feedback was missed,
      double-check that all changes in the pull request address the original requirements of the issue,
      check for newly introduced bugs in the pull request by carefully reading gh pr diff,
      check that no previously existing features were removed without an explicit request in the issue description, issue comments, or pull request comments.
   - When you finish implementation, use gh pr ready 635.

Workflow and collaboration.
   - When you check branch, verify with git branch --show-current.
   - When you push, push only to branch issue-526-215ae32f521b.
   - When you finish, create a pull request from branch issue-526-215ae32f521b. (Note: PR 635 already exists, update it instead)
   - When you organize workflow, use pull requests instead of direct merges to default branch (main or master).
   - When you manage commits, preserve commit history for later analysis.
   - When you contribute, keep repository history forward-moving with regular commits, pushes, and reverts if needed.
   - When you face conflict that you cannot resolve yourself, ask for help.
   - When you collaborate, respect branch protections by working only on issue-526-215ae32f521b.
   - When you mention a result, include the pull request URL or comment URL.
   - When you need to create pr, remember pr 635 already exists for this branch.

Self review.
   - When you check your solution draft, run all tests locally.
   - When you check your solution draft, verify git status shows a clean working tree with no uncommitted changes.
   - When you compare with repo style, use gh pr diff [number].
   - When you finalize, confirm code, tests, and description are consistent.

GitHub CLI command patterns.
   - When fetching lists from GitHub API, use the --paginate flag to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).
   - When listing PR review comments (inline code comments), use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
   - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/\${owner}/\${repo}/pulls/\${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.

Playwright MCP usage (browser automation via mcp__playwright__* tools).
   - When you develop frontend web applications (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Vue, Angular, etc.), use Playwright MCP tools to test the UI in a real browser.
   - When WebFetch tool fails to retrieve expected content (e.g., returns empty content, JavaScript-rendered pages, or login-protected pages), use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for web browsing.
   - When WebSearch tool fails or returns insufficient results, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for internet search.
   - When you need to interact with dynamic web pages that require JavaScript execution, use Playwright MCP tools.
   - When you need to visually verify how a web page looks or take screenshots, use browser_take_screenshot from Playwright MCP.
   - When you need to fill forms, click buttons, or perform user interactions on web pages, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_click, browser_type, browser_fill_form).
   - When you need to test responsive design or different viewport sizes, use browser_resize from Playwright MCP.
   - When you finish using the browser, close it with browser_close to free resources.
   - When reproducing UI bugs, use browser_take_screenshot to capture the problem state before implementing any fix.
   - When fixing UI bugs, take before/after screenshots to provide visual evidence of the fix for human verification.
   - When creating UI tests, save baseline screenshots to the repository for visual regression testing.
   - When verifying UI fixes, compare screenshots to ensure the fix does not introduce unintended visual changes.

Visual UI work and screenshots.
   - When you work on visual UI changes (frontend, CSS, HTML, design), include a render or screenshot of the final result in the pull request description.
   - When you need to show visual results, take a screenshot and save it to the repository (e.g., in a docs/screenshots/ or assets/ folder).
   - When you save screenshots to the repository, use permanent links in the pull request description markdown (e.g., https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/blob/issue-526-215ae32f521b/docs/screenshots/result.png?raw=true).
   - When uploading images, commit them to the branch first, then reference them using the GitHub blob URL format with ?raw=true suffix (works for both public and private repositories).
   - When the visual result is important for review, mention it explicitly in the pull request description with the embedded image.
   - When fixing UI bugs, capture both the \"before\" (problem) and \"after\" (fixed) screenshots as evidence for human verification.
   - When reporting UI bugs, include a screenshot of the problem state to enable visual verification of the fix.
   - When the fix is visual, include side-by-side or sequential comparison of before/after states in the PR description.
   - When possible, create automated visual regression tests to prevent the UI bug from recurring." | jq -c .)

      write unit tests with mocks for easy and quick start.
   - When you test integrations, use existing framework.
   - When you test solution draft, include automated checks in pr.
   - When you write or modify tests, consider setting reasonable timeouts at test, suite, and CI job levels so failures surface quickly instead of hanging.
   - When you see repeated test timeout patterns in CI, investigate the root cause rather than increasing timeouts.
   - When the issue is unclear, write a comment on the issue with questions.
   - When you encounter problems that you cannot solve yourself and need human help, write a comment on the pull request asking for help.
   - When you need human help, use gh pr comment 635 --body "your message" to comment on existing PR.

Reproducible testing.
   - When fixing a bug, create a test that reproduces the problem before implementing the fix. When you cannot reproduce the problem, you cannot verify the fix.
   - When encountering logic bugs, write an automated test that fails due to the bug, then implement the fix to make it pass.
   - When encountering UI bugs, capture a screenshot showing the problem state, then create a visual regression test or manual verification screenshot after the fix.
   - When creating tests, prefer minimum reproducible examples, meaning the simplest test case that demonstrates the issue.
   - When submitting a fix, include in the PR description: (1) how to reproduce the issue, (2) the automated test that verifies the fix, (3) before/after screenshots for UI issues.
   - When a bug fix does not have a reproducing test, treat the fix as incomplete because regressions can occur later without notice.

Preparing pull request.
   - When you code, follow contributing guidelines.
   - When you commit, write clear message.
   - When you need examples of style, use gh pr list --repo link-assistant/formal-ai --state merged --search [keywords].
   - When you open pr, describe solution draft and include tests.
   - When there is a package with version and GitHub Actions workflows for automatic release, update the version (or other necessary release trigger) in your pull request to prepare for next release.
   - When you update existing pr 635, use gh pr edit to modify title and description.
   - When you are about to commit or push code, run local CI checks first if they are available in contributing guidelines (like ruff check, mypy, eslint, etc.) to catch errors before pushing.
   - When you finalize the pull request:
      follow style from merged prs for code, title, and description,
      check that no uncommitted changes corresponding to the original requirements are left behind,
      check that the default branch is merged into the pull request branch,
      check that all CI checks are passing if they exist before you finish,
      check for latest comments on the issue and pull request to ensure no recent feedback was missed,
      double-check that all changes in the pull request address the original requirements of the issue,
      check for newly introduced bugs in the pull request by carefully reading gh pr diff,
      check that no previously existing features were removed without an explicit request in the issue description, issue comments, or pull request comments.
   - When you finish implementation, use gh pr ready 635.

Workflow and collaboration.
   - When you check branch, verify with git branch --show-current.
   - When you push, push only to branch issue-526-215ae32f521b.
   - When you finish, create a pull request from branch issue-526-215ae32f521b. (Note: PR 635 already exists, update it instead)
   - When you organize workflow, use pull requests instead of direct merges to default branch (main or master).
   - When you manage commits, preserve commit history for later analysis.
   - When you contribute, keep repository history forward-moving with regular commits, pushes, and reverts if needed.
   - When you face conflict that you cannot resolve yourself, ask for help.
   - When you collaborate, respect branch protections by working only on issue-526-215ae32f521b.
   - When you mention a result, include the pull request URL or comment URL.
   - When you need to create pr, remember pr 635 already exists for this branch.

Self review.
   - When you check your solution draft, run all tests locally.
   - When you check your solution draft, verify git status shows a clean working tree with no uncommitted changes.
   - When you compare with repo style, use gh pr diff [number].
   - When you finalize, confirm code, tests, and description are consistent.

GitHub CLI command patterns.
   - When fetching lists from GitHub API, use the --paginate flag to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).
   - When listing PR review comments (inline code comments), use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
   - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
   - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
   - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/${owner}/${repo}/pulls/${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.

Playwright MCP usage (browser automation via mcp__playwright__* tools).
   - When you develop frontend web applications (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Vue, Angular, etc.), use Playwright MCP tools to test the UI in a real browser.
   - When WebFetch tool fails to retrieve expected content (e.g., returns empty content, JavaScript-rendered pages, or login-protected pages), use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for web browsing.
   - When WebSearch tool fails or returns insufficient results, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for internet search.
   - When you need to interact with dynamic web pages that require JavaScript execution, use Playwright MCP tools.
   - When you need to visually verify how a web page looks or take screenshots, use browser_take_screenshot from Playwright MCP.
   - When you need to fill forms, click buttons, or perform user interactions on web pages, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_click, browser_type, browser_fill_form).
   - When you need to test responsive design or different viewport sizes, use browser_resize from Playwright MCP.
   - When you finish using the browser, close it with browser_close to free resources.
   - When reproducing UI bugs, use browser_take_screenshot to capture the problem state before implementing any fix.
   - When fixing UI bugs, take before/after screenshots to provide visual evidence of the fix for human verification.
   - When creating UI tests, save baseline screenshots to the repository for visual regression testing.
   - When verifying UI fixes, compare screenshots to ensure the fix does not introduce unintended visual changes.

Visual UI work and screenshots.
   - When you work on visual UI changes (frontend, CSS, HTML, design), include a render or screenshot of the final result in the pull request description.
   - When you need to show visual results, take a screenshot and save it to the repository (e.g., in a docs/screenshots/ or assets/ folder).
   - When you save screenshots to the repository, use permanent links in the pull request description markdown (e.g., https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/blob/issue-526-215ae32f521b/docs/screenshots/result.png?raw=true).
   - When uploading images, commit them to the branch first, then reference them using the GitHub blob URL format with ?raw=true suffix (works for both public and private repositories).
   - When the visual result is important for review, mention it explicitly in the pull request description with the embedded image.
   - When fixing UI bugs, capture both the "before" (problem) and "after" (fixed) screenshots as evidence for human verification.
   - When reporting UI bugs, include a screenshot of the problem state to enable visual verification of the fix.
   - When the fix is visual, include side-by-side or sequential comparison of before/after states in the PR description.
   - When possible, create automated visual regression tests to prevent the UI bug from recurring.

        "content": "title:\tTranslation quality test\nstate:\tOPEN\nauthor:\tkonard (Konstantin Diachenko)\nlabels:\tdocumentation, enhancement\ncomments:\t0\nassignees:\t\nprojects:\t\nmilestone:\t\nissue-type:\tFeature\nparent:\t\nsub-issues:\t\nsub-issues-completed:\t\nblocked-by:\t\nblocking:\t\nnumber:\t526\n--\nThe best translation is the translation that survives round trip translation.\n\nSo we should by default find such formulation for translation, that is more likely and in most part will survive round trip translation.\n\nWe need to make sure that all our logic related to formal translation via meta language (between programming languages, between natural languages) all have round trip translation quality test.\n\nTranslation to meta language from any other language must have zero data loss, meaning, roundtrip from meta language and back must work always.\n\nYet we also need to test roundtrips from Rust to meta language to JavaScript (roundtrip via meta language between JavaScript and Rust), from JavaScript to meta language to Rust (and round trip). The same for Russian -> meta language -> English, English -> meta language -> Russian. So Rust <-> JavaScript, Russian <-> English and so on. We should support tests for all roundtrips between each separate language and meta language, and between each 2 languages we support via meta language.\n\nWe should never support direct translation without meta language, as doing so will be computationally unsolvable.\n\nFor example if we have 100 languages, translators between them will be 100 * 100 = 10 000, and if we have 100 languages + meta language, we will need only 200 translators (to and from meta language from each 100 languages).\n\nMake sure our vision, requirements, roadmap and contributing guide all do not contradict this, and all include all relevant text about the case.\n\nWe need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).\n\nPlease plan and execute everything in this single pull request, you have unlimited time and context, as context auto-compacts and you can continue indefinitely, until it is each and every requirement fully addressed, and everything is totally done.",

      {
        "tool_use_id": "toolu_01KKDppG2NZX3dE4GQryv3jh",
        "type": "tool_result",
        "content": "   607 src/translation/cache.rs\n   795 src/translation/formalization.rs\n    78 src/translation/formatting.rs\n   141 src/translation/http.rs\n    97 src/translation/language_markers.rs\n    98 src/translation/meaning.rs\n   113 src/translation/mod.rs\n   846 src/translation/pipeline.rs\n   234 src/translation/prompt.rs\n   417 src/translation/selection.rs\n   311 src/translation/wikidata.rs\n   685 src/translation/wiktionary.rs\n  4422 total\n---\n//! Translation pipeline.\n//!\n//! Translates an arbitrary natural-language fragment from one language to\n//! another by running the full\n//! `source → formalize → semantic meta language → deformalize → target`\n//! flow on top of Wikipedia, Wikidata and Wiktionary. There is no\n//! pre-extracted translation table built into the binary: every answer is\n//! the result of an actual API round-trip (live or replayed from the\n//! seeded raw-response cache).\n//!\n//! 1. **Formalize** — fetch the source-edition Wiktionary page and the\n//!    Wikidata Lexeme / Q-item that backs the surface so the surface\n//!    collapses to a language-neutral [`MeaningId`].\n//!\n//! 2. **Deformalize** — render that [`MeaningId`] back into the target\n//!    language by joining on Wikidata `P5137` (\"item for this sense\") and\n//!    by parsing translation tables (`{{trans-top}}`, `{{перев-блок}}`,\n//!    `=== Translations ===` / `=== Перевод ===`) on either the source-\n//!    or target-edition Wiktionary page.\n//!\n//! Every successful HTTP response is preserved verbatim under\n//! [`cache::DEFAULT_CACHE_DIR`] keyed by **semantic identity** of the\n//! resource (Wikidata Q-id, Wiktionary `(lang, page)`, SPARQL query hash,\n//! …) so a single fetch can feed translation, fact lookup, attribute\n//! formalization or any other formalization path. Older raw response seed\n//! bundles under [`cache::SEED_CACHE_DIR`] are still replayable when present,\n//! but issue #398 moved reviewed source-id snapshots to lossless `.lino` JSON\n//! files under `data/cache/wikidata/`. Live fetches are gated on\n//! `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1`.\n//!\n//! ## Module layout\n//!\n//! - [`http`] — `curl`-backed HTTP client; mirrors [`crate::telegram_runtime`]\n//!   so we don't pull a TLS crate into the core.\n//! - [`cache`] — semantic-identity file cache for raw API responses, with\n//!   support for replaying responses from a committed `.lino` seed bundle.\n//! - [`meaning`] — [`MeaningId`], the semantic meta-language identity.\n//! - [`wiktionary`] — Wiktionary client + wikitext parser.\n//! - [`wikidata`] — Wikidata SPARQL + Lexeme / entity / property client.\n//! - [`formatting`] — typography mirror (case + terminal punctuation).\n//! - [`pipeline`] — orchestration (`TranslationPipeline::translate`).\n//!\n//! ## Default wiring\n//!\n//! Most callers want a process-wide translator that consults the seeded\n//! raw-response cache first and falls through to live HTTP only when\n//! `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1`. Use [`translate_via_default_pipeline`] for that.\n\nuse std::sync::OnceLock;\n\npub mod cache;\npub mod formalization;\npub mod formatting;\npub mod http;\nmod language_markers;\npub mod meaning;\npub mod pipeline;\npub mod prompt;\npub mod selection;\npub mod wikidata;\npub mod wiktionary;\n\npub use cache::CachedHttpClient;\npub use formalization::{\n    formalize_prompt, formalize_prompt_candidates, FormalizationAnchor, FormalizationAnchorKind,\n    FormalizationCandidate, FormalizationRole, FormalizationSlot,\n};\npub use formatting::match_source_formatting;\npub use http::{CurlClient, HttpError};\npub(crate) use language_markers::{\n    detect_comprehension_failure, detect_response_language, detect_source_language,\n    detect_target_language,\n};\npub(crate) use pipeline::seed_meaning_for_surface;\npub use pipeline::{Translation, TranslationPipeline};\npub use prompt::extract_unquoted_translation_surface;\npub use selection::{\n    formalization_probability_target, select_formalization_candidate,\n    select_formalization_candidate_with_policy,\n    select_formalization_candidate_with_probability_store, softmax_formalization_scores,\n    FormalizationDecision, FormalizationSelection, FormalizationSelectionConfig,\n    FormalizationSelectionReason,\n};\n\n/// Process-wide cached HTTP client used by the default pipeline.\n///\n/// The client reads from the committed seed cache and the gitignored\n/// local accelerator under `data/` first and falls through to the live\n/// network only when `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1` is set. This keeps unit\n/// tests offline by default; integration runs that refresh the cache\n/// opt in explicitly.\nfn default_cached_client() -> &'static CachedHttpClient<CurlClient> {\n    static CLIENT: OnceLock<CachedHttpClient<CurlClient>> = OnceLock::new();\n    CLIENT.get_or_init(|| CachedHttpClient::new(cache::DEFAULT_CACHE_DIR, CurlClient::default()))\n}\n\n/// Translate `surface` from `source` to `target` using the default pipeline.\n///\n/// Uses the process-wide cached translator and returns the primary\n/// candidate surface form along with the meaning id, so callers can\n/// both render the answer and embed the meaning id in their trace.\n///\n/// Errors propagate as [`HttpError`]; zero-candidate translations are\n/// returned as explicit gaps so callers can surface a traceable miss\n/// without manufacturing a target-language placeholder.\npub fn translate_via_default_pipeline(\n    surface: &str,\n    source: &str,\n    target: &str,\n) -> Result<Translation, HttpError> {\n    let pipeline = TranslationPipeline::new(default_cached_client());\n    pipeline.translate(surface, source, target)\n}",
        "is_error": false

in\n854\t    // data/seed/meanings-translation.lino — rather than the hardcoded literals it\n855\t    // used before. The refactor is behaviour-preserving: across the full dispatch\n856\t    // pipeline the `concept_lookup` handler answers these prompts first (the phrase\n857\t    // \"links notation\" names a known concept), so the define-gate's routing never\n858\t    // changes the observable answer. This test locks that public contract so a\n859\t    // future dispatch-order change can't silently alter it; the seed→code wiring of\n860\t    // the gate itself is locked by the lib test\n861\t    // `define_in_links_roles_expose_the_scanned_surfaces` in src/seed/meanings.rs.\n862\t    let cases: &[&str] = &[\n863\t        \"define `apple` in links notation\",\n864\t        \"define \\\"apple\\\" in links notation\",\n865\t        \"define `apple` в links notation\",\n866\t        \"define apple in links notation\",\n867\t    ];\n868\t    for prompt in cases {\n869\t        let response = answer(prompt);\n870\t        assert_eq!(\n871\t            response.intent, \"concept_lookup\",\n872\t            \"define-in-links prompt should resolve to the Links Notation concept for {prompt:?}, got {}: {}\",\n873\t            response.intent, response.answer,\n874\t        );\n875\t        assert!(\n876\t            response.answer.starts_with(\"Links Notation (data-format):\"),\n877\t            \"expected the Links Notation concept definition for {prompt:?}, got: {}\",\n878\t            response.answer,\n879\t        );\n880\t    }\n881\t}\n882\t"

      "filePath": "/tmp/gh-issue-solver-1783171526016/tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs",
      "content": "//! Translation-via-Links tests.\n//!\n//! `VISION.md` argues that Links Notation is the language of meaning: every\n//! human language is a surface form, and translation between languages\n//! happens by first projecting both sentences into the same link network.\n//! These tests pin down that contract for the full-scope.\n\nuse formal_ai::{FormalAiEngine, SymbolicAnswer};\n\nfn answer(prompt: &str) -> SymbolicAnswer {\n    FormalAiEngine.answer(prompt)\n}\n\nfn meaning_link(response: &SymbolicAnswer) -> &str {\n    response\n        .evidence_links\n        .iter()\n        .find(|link| link.starts_with(\"meaning:\"))\n        .unwrap_or_else(|| panic!(\"expected a meaning link, got {:?}\", response.evidence_links))\n}\n\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// Active expectations: implementation already returns a Links Notation trace.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n#[test]\nfn every_answer_publishes_a_links_notation_trace() {\n    let response = answer(\"Hi\");\n    assert!(!response.links_notation.is_empty());\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn russian_translate_how_are_you_prompt_returns_english_surface() {\n    // R213/R214: the answer is the deformalized surface form, preserving the\n    // source fragment's lowercase casing and trailing question mark. No\n    // `meaning: ...` / `surface (...)` template anymore.\n    let response = answer(\"Переведи \\\"как у тебя дела?\\\" на английский.\");\n    assert_eq!(\n        response.intent, \"translate_ru_to_en\",\n        \"Russian translation prompt should resolve to translation, got {}: {}\",\n        response.intent, response.answer,\n    );\n    assert!(\n        response.answer.contains(\"how are you?\"),\n        \"translation should preserve the source's lowercase casing, got: {}\",\n        response.answer,\n    );\n    assert!(\n        !response.answer.contains(\"How are you?\"),\n        \"source was lowercase, so the target must not be capitalized; got: {}\",\n        response.answer,\n    );\n    assert!(\n        !response.answer.contains(\"surface (\"),\n        \"natural translation must not use the robotic surface (lang): template; got: {}\",\n        response.answer,\n    );\n    assert!(\n translation_gaps_are_reported_without_language_placeholders() {\n    let cases: &[(&str, &str, &str, &str, &[&str])] = &[\n        (\n            \"en\",\n            \"Переведи \\\"неведомослово\\\" на английский\",\n            \"translate_ru_to_en\",\n            \"translation_gap:неведомослово\",\n            &[\"[en]\", \"[En]\"],\n        ),\n        (\n            \"ru\",\n            \"Translate \\\"zzqxqv\\\" to Hindi\",\n            \"translate_en_to_hi\",\n            \"translation_gap:zzqxqv\",\n            &[\"[hi]\", \"[Hi]\"],\n        ),\n        (\n   for (language, prompt, expected_intent, expected_gap, forbidden_placeholders) in cases {\n        let response = answer(prompt);\n        assert_eq!(\n            response.intent, *expected_intent,\n            \"translation gap should stay on the translation handler for {prompt:?}, got {}: {}\",\n            response.intent, response.answer,\n        );\n        for placeholder in *forbidden_placeholders {\n            assert!(\n                !response.answer.contains(placeholder),\n                \"translation gap must not render placeholder {placeholder} for {prompt:?}, got: {}\",\n                response.answer,\n            );\n        }\n        assert!(\n            response\n                .answer\n                .to_lowercase()\n                .contains(\"could not translate\"),\n            \"translation gap should be explicit to the user for {prompt:?}, got: {}\",\n            response.answer,\n        );\n        assert!(\n            response\n                .evidence_links\n                .iter()\n                .any(|link| link == expected_gap),\n            \"translation gap must be traceable as {expected_gap}, got {:?}\",\n            response.evidence_links,\n        );\n        assert!(\n            response\n                .evidence_links\n                .iter()\n                .any(|link| link == ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\n#[test]\nfn translation_preserves_meaning_id_across_languages() {\n    let english = answer(\"Translate 'Hello, how are you?' to Russian\");\n    let russian = answer(\"Переведи 'Hello, how are you?' на русский\");\n    assert!(\n        english.intent.starts_with(\"translate_\") && russian.intent.starts_with(\"translate_\"),\n        \"both prompts should be classified as translation requests\"\n    );\n    let english_meaning_id = english\n        .evidence_links\n        .iter()\n        .find(|link| link.starts_with(\"meaning:\"));\n    let russian_meaning_id = russian\n        .evidence_links\n        .iter()\n        .find(|link| link.starts_with(\"meaning:\"));\n    assert!(english_meaning_id.is_some() && english_meaning_id == russian_meaning_id);\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn translation_request_returns_target_surface_form() {\n    let response = answer(\"Translate rust_javascript_code_translation_round_trips_through_code_meaning() {\n    let rust_source = \"fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { a + b }\";\n    let rust_to_js = answer(&format!(\n        \"Translate `{rust_source}` from Rust to JavaScript\"\n    ));\n    assert_eq!(\n        rust_to_js.intent, \"translate_rust_to_javascript\",\n        \"Rust->JavaScript code translation should route through the         "content": "1\t//! Round-trip translation tests (issue #526).\n2\t//!\n3\t//! Issue #526 states the quality bar for translation: *\"the best translation\n4\t//! is the translation that survives round-trip translation.\"* A faithful\n5\t//! translation `A --(source→target)--> B` must translate back\n6\t//! `B --(target→source)--> A` to the *same meaning* — and, for a well-seeded\n7\t//! vocabulary, the *same surface* it started from.\n8\t//!\n9\t//! These tests exercise the real\n10\t//! `source → formalize → semantic meta language → deformalize → target`\n11\t//! pipeline through [`translate_via_default_pipeline`], entirely offline\n12\t//! against the committed seed cache (no `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API`). They assert the\n13\t//! two round-trip invariants that matter:\n14\t//!\n15\t//! 1. **Meaning survives** — the language-neutral [`MeaningId`] of the forward\n16\t//!    translation equals the meaning of the backward translation. Translation\n17\t//!    passes *through* the meta language, so the meta-language identity is the\n18\t//!    invariant the surfaces are grounded in.\n19\t//! 2. **Surface survives** — the backward surface equals the original surface\n20\t//!    (case-insensitively). This is the observable #526 criterion.\n21\t//!\n22\t//! The pipeline holds no hardcoded phrase table: every surface here is grounded\n23\t//! in `data/seed/meanings-*.lino` and the seeded Wiktionary/Wikidata cache, so\n24\t//! widening the covered vocabulary is a pure seed edit.\n25\t\n26\tuse formal_ai::translation::translate_via_default_pipeline;\n27\t\n28\t#[derive(Clone, Copy)]\n29\tstruct SurfaceCase {\n30\t    language: &'static str,\n31\t    surface: &'static str,\n32\t}\n33\t\n34\tconst APPLE_SURFACES: &[SurfaceCase] = &[\n35\t    SurfaceCase {\n36\t        language: \"en\",\n37\t        surface: \"apple\",\n38\t    },\n39\t    SurfaceCase {\n40\t        language: \"ru\",\n41\t        surface: \"яблоко\",\n42\t    },\n43\t    SurfaceCase {\n44\t        language: \"hi\",\n45\t        surface: \"सेब\",\n46\t    },\n47\t    SurfaceCase {\n48\t        language: \"zh\",\n49\t        surface: \"苹果\",\n50\t    },\n51\t];\n52\t\n53\t/// Assert the full #526 round-trip for one surface across one language pair:\n54\t/// `source --A→B--> target --B→A--> source`, preserving meaning and surface.\n55\tfn assert_round_trip(surface: &str, source: &str, target: &str) {\n56\t    let forward = translate_via_default_pipeline(surface, source, target).unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n57\t        panic!(\"forward {surface:?} {source}->{target} should translate offline, got {error:?}\")\n58\t    });\n59\t    let target_surface = forward.primary_surface().unwrap_or_else(|| {\n60\t        panic!(\"forward {surface:?} {source}->{target} should yield a target surface\")\n61\t    });\n62\t    assert_ne!(\n63\t        target_surface.to_lowercase(),\n64\t        surface.to_lowercase(),\n65\t        \"forward {surface:?} {source}->{target} should change the surface, got {target_surface:?}\",\n66\t    );\n67\t\n68\t    let backward =\n69\t        translate_via_default_pipeline(target_surface, target, source).unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n70\t            panic!(\n71\t the {source}->{target}->{source} round trip, \\\n94\t         got {surface:?} -> {target_surface:?} -> {round_tripped:?}\",\n95\t    );\n96\t}\n97\t\n98\tfn assert_surface_eq(left: &str, right: &str, message: &str) {\n99\t    assert_eq!(\n100\t        left.to_lowercase(),\n101\t        right.to_lowercase(),\n102\t        \"{message}: expected {right:?}, got {left:?}\",\n103\t    );\n104\t}\n105\t\n106\t#[test]\n107\tfn english_russian_vocabulary_survives_round_trip() {\n108\t    for surface in [\"hello\", \"apple\", \"thank you\", \"water\", \"bread\"] {\n109\t       "filePath": "/tmp/gh-issue-solver-1783171526016/tests/unit/specification/translation_round_trip.rs",
      "content": "//! Round-trip translation tests (issue #526).\n//!\n//! Issue #526 states the quality bar for translation: *\"the best translation\n//! is the translation that survives round-trip translation.\"* A faithful\n//! translation `A --(source→target)--> B` must translate back\n//! `B --(target→source)--> A` to the *same meaning* — and, for a well-seeded\n//! vocabulary, the *same surface* it started from.\n//!\n//! These tests exercise the real\n//! `source → formalize → semantic meta language → deformalize → target`\n//! pipeline through [`translate_via_default_pipeline`], entirely offline\n//! against the committed seed cache (no `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API`). They assert the\n//! two round-trip invariants that matter:\n//!\n//! 1. **Meaning survives** — the language-neutral [`MeaningId`] of the forward\n//!    translation equals the meaning of the backward translation. Translation\n//!    passes *through* the meta language, so the meta-language identity is the\n//!    invariant the surfaces are grounded in.\n//! 2. **Surface survives** — the backward surface equals the original surface\n//!    (case-insensitively). This is the observable #526 criterion.\n//!\n//! The pipeline holds no hardcoded phrase table: every surface here is grounded\n//! in `data/seed/meanings-*.lino` and the seeded Wiktionary/Wikidata cache, so\n//! widening the covered vocabulary is a pure seed edit.\n\nuse formal_ai::translation::translate_via_default_pipeline;\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy)]\nstruct SurfaceCase {\n    language: &'static str,\n    surface: &'static str,\n}\n\nconst APPLE_SURFACES: &[SurfaceCase] = &[\n    SurfaceCase {\n        language: \"en\",\n        surface: \"apple\",\n    },\n    SurfaceCase {\n        language: \"ru\",\n        surface: \"яблоко\",\n    },\n    SurfaceCase {\n        language: \"hi\",\n        surface: \"सेब\",\n    },\n    SurfaceCase {\n        language: \"zh\",\n        surface: \"苹果\",\n    },\n];\n\n/// Assert the full #526 round-trip for one surface across one language pair:\n/// `source --A→B--> target --B→A--> source`, preserving meaning and surface.\nfn assert_round_trip(surface: &str, source: &str, target: &str) {\n    let forward = translate_via_default_pipeline(surface, source, target).unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n        panic!(\"forward {surface:?} {source}->{target} should translate offline, got {error:?}\")\n    });\n    let target_surface = forward.primary_surface().unwrap_or_else(|| {\n        panic!(\"forward {surface:?} {source}->{target} should yield a target surface\")\n    });\n    assert_ne!(\n        target_surface.to_lowercase(),\n        surface.to_lowercase(),\n        \"forward {surface:?} {source}->{target} should change the surface, got {target_surface:?}\",\n    );\n\n    let backward =\n        translate_via_default_pipeline(target_surface, target, source).unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n            panic!(\n                \"backward {target_surface:?} {target}->{source} should translate offline, \\\n                 got {error:?}\"\n            )\n        });\n    let round_tripped = backward.primary_surface().unwrap_or_else(|| {\n        panic!(\"backward {target_surface:?} {target}->{source} should yield a surface\")\n    });\n\n    // 1. Meaning survives the round trip — the meta-language identity is stable.\n    assert_eq!(\n        forward.meaning.slug(),\n        backward.meaning.slug(),\n        \"#526: meaning must survive the {source}->{target}->{source} round trip for {surface:?} \\\n         (forward {} vs backward {})\",\n        forward.meaning.slug(),\n        backward.meaning.slug(),\n    );\n\n    // 2. Surface survives — the observable #526 criterion.\n    assert_eq!(\n        round_tripped.to_lowercase(),\n        surface.to_lowercase(),\n        \"#526: {surface:?} should survive the {source}->{target}->{source} round trip, \\\n         got {surface:?} -> {target_surface:?} -> {round_tripped:?}\",\n    );\n}\n\nfn assert_surface_eq(left: &str, right: &str, message: &str) {\n    assert_eq!(\n        left.to_lowercase(),\n        right.to_lowercase(),\n        \"{message}: expected {right:?}, got {left:?}\",\n    );\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn english_russian_vocabulary_survives_round_trip() {\n    for surface in [\"hello\", \"apple\", \"thank you\", \"water\", \"bread\"] {\n        assert_round_trip(surface, \"en\", \"ru\");\n    }\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn english_hindi_vocabulary_survives_round_trip() {\n    for surface in [\"hello\", \"apple\"] {\n        assert_round_trip(surface, \"en\", \"hi\");\n    }\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn english_chinese_vocabulary_survives_round_trip() {\n    for surface in [\"hello\", \"apple\"] {\n        assert_round_trip(surface, \"en\", \"zh\");\n    }\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn supported_language_surfaces_survive_meta_language_round_trip() {\n    for case in APPLE_SURFACES {\n        let round_trip = translate_via_default_pipeline(case.surface, case.language, case.language)\n            .unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n                panic!(\n                    \"#526: {:?} ({}) should formalize to the meta language and deformalize \\\n                     back without data loss, got {error:?}\",\n                    case.surface, case.language\n                )\n            });\n        let surface = round_trip.primary_surface().unwrap_or_else(|| {\n            panic!(\n                \"#526: {:?} ({}) should deformalize from the meta language\",\n                case.surface, case.language\n            )\n        });\n        assert_surface_eq(\n            surface,\n            case.surface,\n            \"#526: language-to-meta-to-same-language must be lossless\",\n        );\n        assert_ne!(\n            round_trip.meaning.slug(),\n            \"meaning:unknown\",\n            \"#526: {:?} ({}) must carry a traceable meta-language meaning\",\n            case.surface,\n            case.language,\n        );\n    }\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn every_supported_language_pair_round_trips_via_meta_language() {\n    for source in APPLE_SURFACES {\n        for target in APPLE_SURFACES {\n            if source.language == target.language {\n                continue;\n            }\n\n            let forward =\n                translate_via_default_pipeline(source.surface, source.language, target.language)\n                    .unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n                        panic!(\n                            \"#526: forward {:?} {}->{} should translate through the meta \\\n                             language, got {error:?}\",\n                            source.surface, source.language, target.language\n                        )\n                    });\n            let target_surface = forward.primary_surface().unwrap_or_else(|| {\n                panic!(\n                    \"#526: forward {:?} {}->{} should yield a target surface\",\n                    source.surface, source.language, target.language\n                )\n            });\n            assert_surface_eq(\n                target_surface,\n                target.surface,\n                \"#526: forward translation should render the target-language surface\",\n            );\n\n            let backward =\n                translate_via_default_pipeline(target_surface, target.language, source.language)\n                    .unwrap_or_else(|error| {\n                        panic!(\n                            \"#526: backward {:?} {}->{} should translate through the meta \\\n                             language, got {error:?}\",\n                            target_surface, target.language, source.language\n                        )\n                    });\n            let round_tripped = backward.primary_surface().unwrap_or_else(|| {\n                panic!(\n                    \"#526: backward {:?} {}->{} should yield the original-language surface\",\n                    target_surface, target.language, source.language\n                )\n            });\n\n            assert_eq!(\n                forward.meaning.slug(),\n                backward.meaning.slug(),\n                \"#526: meaning id must survive {}->{}->{} for {:?}\",\n                source.language,\n                target.language,\n                source.language,\n                source.surface,\n            );\n            assert_surface_eq(\n                round_tripped,\n                source.surface,\n                \"#526: every supported language pair must round-trip through the meta language\",\n            );\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// The forward and backward legs must agree on the *same* meta-language\n/// identity for every seeded target language — one meaning, many surfaces.\n/// This is the property that lets a synonym in any language collapse to the\n/// same node before the surface is chosen.\n#[test]\nfn one_meaning_backs_every_language_surface() {\n    // Translating the same English noun into each seeded target must resolve to\n    // one and the same meta-language meaning — the surfaces differ, the meaning\n    // does not. This is the property that lets synonyms across languages\n    // collapse to a single node before a surface is chosen.\n    let mut shared_meaning: Option<String> = None;\n    for target in [\"ru\", \"hi\", \"zh\"] {\n        let forward = translate_via_default_pipeline(\"apple\", \"en\", target)\n            .unwrap_or_else(|error| panic!(\"apple en->{target} should translate, got {error:?}\"));\n        let meaning = forward.meaning.slug();\n        match &shared_meaning {\n            None => shared_meaning = Some(meaning),\n            Some(expected) => assert_eq!(\n                &meaning, expected,\n                \"apple should carry one meta-language meaning across every target language, \\\n                 got {meaning} for {target} vs {expected}\",\n            ),\n        }\n    }\n}\n",

A lowercase `как у тебя дела?` should round-trip to lowercase `how are you?`, and a source fragment with no terminal mark should not gain one in the target language. The pipeline is `formalize → meaning → deformalize → match_source_formatting`, and the meaning ID, source language, and target language remain in the Links Notation trace so the translation stays inspectable.\n \n+Issue #526 makes round-trip survival the translation quality contract. Every\n+supported language must pass a language-to-meta-to-same-language check with no\n+data loss, and every supported language pair must translate through the shared\n+meaning before rendering a target surface. Direct pair-specific translation can\n+only be an implementation detail below that contract; it is not acceptable as a\n+quality path when it bypasses the meta language. The same rule applies to code:\n+a Rust <-> JavaScript translation must preserve the code meaning link, not only\n+produce syntax that looks plausible.\n+\n ## Data Is The Interface\n \n The shell code should be about *interfacing* (rendering chat, dispatching tools, persisting events) and not about *logic*. The agent's identity, its multilingual responses, its concept table, and its registered tools should all live in seeded Links Notation files inside an associative store, so a user can fully reconfigure the agent — add a new language, retire an answer, register a new tool, change a rule — by editing data, not by rewriting code. The same principle applies to executable knowledge: precompiled handlers can be seeded, and dynamically-compiled Rust, JavaScript, and WebAssembly snippets can be linked into the store on demand, so the data graph itself defines what the agent can do.",

The case study\n+and online research live in `docs/case-studies/issue-526/`.\n+\n ## Verification Contract\n \n When any roadmap item changes, the PR should update the corresponding rows in\ndiff --git a/VISION.md b/VISION.md\nindex 073f9d5d..776998e9 100644\n--- a/VISION.md\n+++ b/VISION.md\n@@ -187,6 +187,15 @@ Natural languages and programming languages should be translated through link-na\n \n Cross-language translation should also preserve the source's surface signal: the leading capitalization and the terminal punctuation a user typed are part of the meaning, not noise. A lowercase `как у тебя дела?` should round-trip to lowercase `how are you?`, and a source fragment with no terminal mark should not gain one in the target language. The pipeline is `formalize → meaning → deformalize → match_source_formatting`, and the meaning ID, source language, and target language remain in the Links Notation trace so the translation stays inspectable.\n \n+Issue #526 makes round-trip survival the translation quality contract. Every\n+supported language must pass a language-to-meta-to-same-language check with no\n+data loss, and every supported language pair must translate through the shared\n+meaning before rendering a target surface. Direct pair-specific translation can\n+only be an implementation detail below that contract; it is not acceptable as a\n+quality path when it bypasses the meta language. The same rule applies to code:\n+a Rust <-> JavaScript translation must preserve the code meaning link, not only\n+produce syntax that looks plausible.\n+\n ## Data Is The Interface\n \n The shell code should be about *interfacing* (rendering chat, dispatching tools, persisting events) and not about *logic*. The agent's identity, its multilingual responses, its concept table, and its registered tools should all live in seeded Links Notation files inside an associative store, so a user can fully reconfigure the agent — add a new language, retire an answer, register a new tool, change a rule — by editing data, not by rewriting code. The same principle applies to executable knowledge: precompiled handlers can be seeded, and dynamically-compiled Rust, JavaScript, and WebAssembly snippets can be linked into the store on demand, so the data graph itself defines what the agent can do.",

          "new_string": "/// Language-neutral meta representation of a code fragment — the code meta\n/// language.\n///\n/// Issue #526 forbids direct pair-specific translation: with `N` languages a\n/// direct table needs `N * N` translators, which is computationally\n/// intractable. Code translation therefore mirrors the natural-language\n/// pipeline. A fragment is first *formalized* into a `CodeMeaning` and then\n/// *rendered* into the requested target, so the translator stays at `N`\n/// formalizers plus `N` renderers instead of `N * N` hardcoded pairs. Adding a\n/// language is one new [`formalize_code_meaning`] recognizer and one new\n/// [`render_code_meaning`] arm — never a new `(source, target)` pair.\n#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]\npub enum CodeMeaning {\n    /// A binary function `add(a, b)` that returns the sum of its two\n    /// parameters. This is the currently seeded code meaning; widen coverage by\n    /// adding variants here rather than by adding direct language pairs.\n    BinaryAddFunction,\n    /// The fragment has not been formalized into a known code meaning. Carries\n    /// the trimmed source so callers render a traceable gap instead of\n    /// manufacturing an incorrect translation.\n    Unformalized(String),\n}\n\nimpl CodeMeaning {\n    /// Stable, language-neutral slug for this code meaning. Two fragments that\n    /// share a meaning (e.g. the same add function written in Rust and in\n    /// JavaScript) collapse to the same slug, which is what lets a round trip\n    /// preserve its `meaning:` link.\n    pub fn slug(&self) -> String {\n        match self {\n            Self::BinaryAddFunction => String::from(\"function:add:binary_sum\"),\n            Self::Unformalized(source) => source\n                .chars()\n                .filter(char::is_ascii_alphanumeric)\n                .collect::<String>()\n                .to_lowercase(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// Formalize a source-language code fragment into the language-neutral code\n/// meta language. The source language is irrelevant to the result: `add`\n/// written in Rust, Python, or JavaScript all collapse to the same\n/// [`CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction`], which is exactly why the round trip\n/// preserves meaning.\npub fn formalize_code_meaning(code: &str) -> CodeMeaning {\n    if is_binary_add_function(code) {\n        CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction\n    } else {\n        CodeMeaning::Unformalized(code.trim().to_owned())\n    }\n}\n\n/// Render a [`CodeMeaning`] into `target`'s surface syntax. When the meaning is\n/// unknown, or the target language has no seeded rendering yet, this returns a\n/// traceable, language-appropriate translation-gap comment rather than\n/// inventing plausible-but-wrong code.\npub fn render_code_meaning(meaning: &CodeMeaning, source: &str, target: &str) -> String {\n    if let CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction = meaning {\n        if let Some(rendered) = render_binary_add(target) {\n            return rendered;\n        }\n    }\n    let subject = match meaning {\n        CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction => \"add function\",\n        CodeMeaning::Unformalized(source_code) => source_code.as_str(),\n    };\n    format!(\n        \"{} translation gap for `{subject}` from {source} to {target}\",\n        code_comment_prefix(target)\n    )\n}\n\n/// Render the seeded [`CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction`] into `target`. Returns\n/// `None` when `target` has no seeded rendering, so the caller can emit a gap.\nfn render_binary_add(target: &str) -> Option<String> {\n    let rendered = match target {\n        \"python\" => \"def add(a, b):\\n    return a + b\",\n        \"rust\" => \"fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {\\n    a + b\\n}\",\n        \"javascript\" => \"function add(a, b) {\\n    return a + b;\\n}\",\n        \"typescript\" => \"function add(a: number, b: number): number {\\n    return a + b;\\n}\",\n        \"go\" => \"func add(a int, b int) int {\\n    return a + b\\n}\",\n        _ => return None,\n    };\n    Some(String::from(rendered))\n}\n\n/// Comment prefix used to render a translation gap in `language`'s own syntax.\nfn code_comment_prefix(language: &str) -> &'static str {\n    match language {\n        \"python\" | \"ruby\" => \"#\",\n        _ => \"//\",\n    }\n}\n\n/// Translate a code fragment from `source` to `target` by routing through the\n/// code meta language: `source -> CodeMeaning -> target`. There is no direct\n/// `(source, target)` path — see [`CodeMeaning`] for why.\npub fn translate_program(code: &str, source: &str, target: &str) -> String {\n    let meaning = formalize_code_meaning(code);\n    render_code_meaning(&meaning, source, target)\n}\n\n/// Language-neutral meaning slug for a code fragment, used to key its\n/// `meaning:` evidence link. Delegates to [`formalize_code_meaning`] so the\n/// meaning a fragment translates *through* is the same meaning its trace\n/// records — that shared identity is the #526 round-trip invariant.\npub fn normalize_code_meaning(code: &str) -> String {\n    formalize_code_meaning(code).slug()\n}\n\nfn is_binary_add_function(code: &str) -> bool {"

    "newString": "/// Language-neutral meta representation of a code fragment — the code meta\n/// language.\n///\n/// Issue #526 forbids direct pair-specific translation: with `N` languages a\n/// direct table needs `N * N` translators, which is computationally\n/// intractable. Code translation therefore mirrors the natural-language\n/// pipeline. A fragment is first *formalized* into a `CodeMeaning` and then\n/// *rendered* into the requested target, so the translator stays at `N`\n/// formalizers plus `N` renderers instead of `N * N` hardcoded pairs. Adding a\n/// language is one new [`formalize_code_meaning`] recognizer and one new\n/// [`render_code_meaning`] arm — never a new `(source, target)` pair.\n#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]\npub enum CodeMeaning {\n    /// A binary function `add(a, b)` that returns the sum of its two\n    /// parameters. This is the currently seeded code meaning; widen coverage by\n    /// adding variants here rather than by adding direct language pairs.\n    BinaryAddFunction,\n    /// The fragment has not been formalized into a known code meaning. Carries\n    /// the trimmed source so callers render a traceable gap instead of\n    /// manufacturing an incorrect translation.\n    Unformalized(String),\n}\n\nimpl CodeMeaning {\n    /// Stable, language-neutral slug for this code meaning. Two fragments that\n    /// share a meaning (e.g. the same add function written in Rust and in\n    /// JavaScript) collapse to the same slug, which is what lets a round trip\n    /// preserve its `meaning:` link.\n    pub fn slug(&self) -> String {\n        match self {\n            Self::BinaryAddFunction => String::from(\"function:add:binary_sum\"),\n            Self::Unformalized(source) => source\n                .chars()\n                .filter(char::is_ascii_alphanumeric)\n                .collect::<String>()\n                .to_lowercase(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// Formalize a source-language code fragment into the language-neutral code\n/// meta language. The source language is irrelevant to the result: `add`\n/// written in Rust, Python, or JavaScript all collapse to the same\n/// [`CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction`], which is exactly why the round trip\n/// preserves meaning.\npub fn formalize_code_meaning(code: &str) -> CodeMeaning {\n    if is_binary_add_function(code) {\n        CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction\n    } else {\n        CodeMeaning::Unformalized(code.trim().to_owned())\n    }\n}\n\n/// Render a [`CodeMeaning`] into `target`'s surface syntax. When the meaning is\n/// unknown, or the target language has no seeded rendering yet, this returns a\n/// traceable, language-appropriate translation-gap comment rather than\n/// inventing plausible-but-wrong code.\npub fn render_code_meaning(meaning: &CodeMeaning, source: &str, target: &str) -> String {\n    if let CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction = meaning {\n        if let Some(rendered) = render_binary_add(target) {\n            return rendered;\n        }\n    }\n    let subject = match meaning {\n        CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction => \"add function\",\n        CodeMeaning::Unformalized(source_code) => source_code.as_str(),\n    };\n    format!(\n        \"{} translation gap for `{subject}` from {source} to {target}\",\n        code_comment_prefix(target)\n    )\n}\n\n/// Render the seeded [`CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction`] into `target`. Returns\n/// `None` when `target` has no seeded rendering, so the caller can emit a gap.\nfn render_binary_add(target: &str) -> Option<String> {\n    let rendered = match target {\n        \"python\" => \"def add(a, b):\\n    return a + b\",\n        \"rust\" => \"fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {\\n    a + b\\n}\",\n        \"javascript\" => \"function add(a, b) {\\n    return a + b;\\n}\",\n        \"typescript\" => \"function add(a: number, b: number): number {\\n    return a + b;\\n}\",\n        \"go\" => \"func add(a int, b int) int {\\n    return a + b\\n}\",\n        _ => return None,\n    };\n    Some(String::from(rendered))\n}\n\n/// Comment prefix used to render a translation gap in `language`'s own syntax.\nfn code_comment_prefix(language: &str) -> &'static str {\n    match language {\n        \"python\" | \"ruby\" => \"#\",\n        _ => \"//\",\n    }\n}\n\n/// Translate a code fragment from `source` to `target` by routing through the\n/// code meta language: `source -> CodeMeaning -> target`. There is no direct\n/// `(source, target)` path — see [`CodeMeaning`] for why.\npub fn translate_program(code: &str, source: &str, target: &str) -> String {\n    let meaning = formalize_code_meaning(code);\n    render_code_meaning(&meaning, source, target)\n}\n\n/// Language-neutral meaning slug for a code fragment, used to key its\n/// `meaning:` evidence link. Delegates to [`formalize_code_meaning`] so the\n/// meaning a fragment translates *through* is the same meaning its trace\n/// records — that shared identity is the #526 round-trip invariant.\npub fn normalize_code_meaning(code: &str) -> String {\n    formalize_code_meaning(code).slug()\n}\n\nfn is_binary_add_function(code: &str) -> bool {",
    "originalFile": "//! Free-function helpers extracted from `solver.rs` to keep that module under\n//! the 1000-line cap enforced by `scripts/check-file-size.rs`. prompt: &str,\n    max_depth: u8,\n) -> Vec<DecomposedSubImpulse> {\n    if max_depth == 0 {\n        return Vec::new();\n    }\n\n    let language = detect_language(prompt);\n    let whole_intent = formalize_intent(prompt, language.slug(), None);\n    if whole_intent.route.is_none() || whole_intent.kind == IntentKind::Courtesy {\n        let independent_parts = independent_actionable_segments(prompt);\n        if independent_parts.len() > 1 {\n            return record_sub_impulses(log, independent_parts, true);\n        }\n    }\n\n    let lower = prompt.to_lowercase();\n    let triggers = [\" and \", \" with tests\", \" with benchmarks\", \"; \"];\n    if !triggers.iter().any(|trigger| lower.contains(trigger)) {\n        return Vec::new();\n independent_actionable_segments(prompt: &str) -> Vec<String> {\n    let parts = split_candidate_actionable_parts(prompt);\n    if parts.len() <= 1\n        || !parts\n            .iter()\n            .all(|part| looks_like_independent_impulse(part))\n    {\n        return Vec::new();\n    }\n    parts\n}\n\nfn split_candidate_actionable_parts(text: &str) -> Vec<String> {\n    let mut parts = Vec::new();\n    for sentence in split_sentences(text) {\n        for clause in sentence.split(';') {\n            for comma_part in clause.split(',') {\n                for and_part in comma_part.split(\" and \") {\n                    let trimmed = strip_leading_coordinator(and_part.trim());\n                    if !trimmed.is_empty() {\n                        parts.push(trimmed.to_owned());\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    }\n    parts\n}\n\nfn split_sentences(text: &str) -> Vec<String> {\n    let chars: Vec<char> = text.chars().collect();\n    let mut sentences = Vec::new();\n    let mut current = String::new();\n    for (index, &ch) in chars.iter().enumerate() {\n        current.push(ch);\n        let strong_terminator = matches!(ch, '?' | '!' | '。' | '！' | '？');\n        let period_boundary =\n            ch == '.' && chars.get(index + 1).is_none_or(|next| next.is_whitespace());\n        if strong_terminator || period_boundary {\n            push_trimmed_segment(&mut sentences, &current);\n            current.clear();\n        }\n    }\n    push_trimmed_segment(&mut sentences, &current);\n    sentences\n}\n\nfn push_trimmed_segment(out: &mut Vec<String>, candidate: &str) {\n    let trimmed = candidate.trim();\n    if !trimmed.is_empty() {\n        out.push(trimmed.to_owned());\n    }\n}\n\nfn strip_leading_coordinator(text: &str) -> &str {\n    let trimmed = text.trim_start();\n    let lowered = trimmed.to_ascii_lowercase();\n    for coordinator in [\"and\", \"then\"] {\n        if lowered == coordinator {\n            return \"\";\n        }\n        let prefix = format!(\"{coordinator} \");\n        if lowered.starts_with(&prefix) {\n            return trimmed[prefix.len()..].trim_start();\n        }\n    }\n    trimmed\n}\n\nfn looks_like_independent_impulse(segment: &str) -> bool {\n    let normalized = normalize_prompt(segment);\n    if normalized.is_empty() {\n        return false;\n    }\n    let language = detect_language(segment);\n    let formalization = formalize_intent(segment, language.slug(), None);\n    formalization.route.is_some()\n        || matches!(\n            formalization.kind,\n            IntentKind::Task\n                | IntentKind::Question\n relevant.starts_with(\"handler:\"))\n}\n\npub fn record_candidates(log: &mut EventLog, prompt: &str, intent: &str) {\n    let lower = prompt.to_lowercase();\n    if lower.contains(\"suggest a name\") || lower.contains(\"suggest names\") {\n        for candidate in [\"LinkLight\", \"Doublet\", \"FormalLeaf\"] {\n            log.append(\"candidate\", candidate.to_owned());\n        }\n \"secondary\".to_owned());\n        return;\n    }\n    log.append(\"candidate\", intent.to_owned());\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug, Clone)]\npub struct ValidationChoice {\n    pub answer: String,\n}\n\npub fn record_validation(log: &mut EventLog, prompt: &str) -> Option<ValidationChoice> {\n    let lower = prompt.to_lowercase();\n    if lower.contains(\"prime\") {\n        if let Some((low, high)) = extract_range(&lower) {\n            for candidate in low..=high {\n                if is_prime(candidate) {\n                    let answer = format!(\"{candidate}\");\n                    log.append(\"validation\", format!(\"prime_between_{low}_and_{high}\"));\n                    return Some(ValidationChoice { answer });\n                }\n            }\n        }\n        log.append(\"validation\", \"no_prime_in_range\".to_owned());\n    }\n    None\n}\n\npub fn extract_range(lower: &str) -> Option<(u64, u64)> {\n    let numbers: Vec<u64> = lower\n        .split(|character: char| !character.is_ascii_digit())\n        .filter_map(|token| token.parse::<u64>().ok())\n        .collect();\n    match numbers.as_slice() {\n        [low, high] if low <= high => Some((*low, *high)),\n        _ => None,\n    }\n}\n\npub const fn is_prime(value: u64) -> bool {\n    if value < 2 {\n        return false;\n    }\n    let mut divisor: u64 = 2;\n    while divisor.saturating_mul(divisor) <= value {\n        if value.is_multiple_of(divisor) {\n            return false;\n        }\n        divisor += 1;\n    }\n    true\n}\n\npub fn extract_quoted_phrase(text: &str) -> Option<String> {\n    for (open, close) in [('\\'', '\\''), ('\"', '\"'), ('`', '`'), ('«', '»')] {\n        if let Some(start) = text.find(open) {\n            if let Some(end_offset) = text[start + open.len_utf8()..].find(close) {\n                let inner = &text[start + open.len_utf8()..start + open.len_utf8() + end_offset];\n                return Some(inner.to_owned());\n            }\n        }\n    }\n    None\n}\n\npub fn extract_backticked(text: &str) -> Option<String> {\n    let start = text.find('`')?;\n    let rest = &text[start + 1..];\n    let end = rest.find('`')?;\n    Some(rest[..end].to_owned())\n}\n\n/// Walk the event log for a user-introduced name. Looks in the current\n/// prompt first, then in each `prior_turn:user` event so name recall works\n/// across multi-turn conversations.\npub fn recall_name_from_history(log: &EventLog, prompt: &str) -> Option<String> {\n    if let Some(name) = extract_introduced_name(prompt) {\n        return Some(name);\n    }\n    for event in log.events() {\n        if event.kind == \"prior_turn:user\" {\n            if let Some(name) = extract_introduced_name(&event.payload) {\n                return Some(name);\n            }\n        }\n    }\n    None\n}\n\n/// Return the last user turn recorded in the log, ignoring the current\n/// impulse. Used by \"what did I just ask?\" style recall handlers.\npub fn last_user_turn(log: &EventLog) -> Option<&str> {\n    log.events()\n        .iter()\n        .rev()\n        .find(|event| event.kind == \"prior_turn:user\")\n        .map(|event| event.payload.as_str())\n}\n\n/// Return the last assistant turn recorded in the log. Used by follow-up\n/// handlers such as \"how it works?\" that need to infer the topic from the\n/// previous reply.\npub fn last_assistant_turn(log: &EventLog) -> Option<&str> {\n    log.events()\n        .iter()\n        .rev()\n        .find(|event| event.kind == \"prior_turn:assistant\")\n        .map(|event| event.payload.as_str())\n}\n\n/// Return the most recent prior turn (regardless of role) together with its\n/// role label, ignoring the current impulse. Used by the \"what was written in\n/// the previous message?\" recall handler, which replays the immediately\n/// preceding message whether it came from the user or the assistant.\npub fn last_turn(log: &EventLog) -> Option<(&'static str, &str)> {\n    log.events()\n        .iter()\n        .rev()\n        .find_map(|event| match event.kind {\n            \"prior_turn:user\" => Some((\"user\", event.payload.as_str())),\n            \"prior_turn:assistant\" => Some((\"assistant\", event.payload.as_str())),\n            _ => None,\n        })\n}\n\npub fn extract_introduced_name(prompt: &str) -> Option<String> {\n    let needles = [\"my name is\", \"i am called\", \"call me\", \"i'm\", \"i am \"];\n    let lower = prompt.to_lowercase();\n    for needle in needles {\n        let mut search_from = 0;\n        while let Some(offset) = lower[search_from..].find(needle) {\n            let absolute = search_from + offset + needle.len();\n            let tail = &prompt[absolute..];\n            let token = tail\n                .trim_start()\n                .split(|c: char| {\n                    c.is_whitespace() || matches!(c, '.' | ',' | '!' | '?' | ';' | ':' | '\\n')\n                })\n                .find(|token| !token.is_empty())?;\n            let cleaned = token.trim_matches(|c: char| !c.is_alphanumeric());\n            if !cleaned.is_empty() && cleaned.chars().next().is_some_and(char::is_alphabetic) {\n                return Some(cleaned.to_owned());\n            }\n            search_from = absolute;\n        }\n    }\n    None\n}\n\npub fn detect_program_languages(normalized: &str) -> Option<(&'static str, &'static str)> {\n    let langs = [\n        \"python\",\n        \"rust\",\n        \"javascript\",\n        \"typescript\",\n        \"go\",\n        \"java\",\n        \"c\",\n        \"ruby\",\n    ];\n    let from = langs\n        .iter()\n        .find(|lang| normalized.contains(&format!(\"from {lang}\")))\n        .copied();\n    let to = langs\n        .iter()\n        .find(|lang| normalized.contains(&format!(\"to {lang}\")))\n        .copied();\n    match (from, to) {\n        (Some(f), Some(t)) => Some((f, t)),\n        _ => None,\n    }\n}\n\npub fn translate_program(code: &str, source: &str, target: &str) -> String {\n    let trimmed = code.trim();\n    let is_binary_add =
