meta-language meaning to the original source surface.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R526-1 | Translation quality must be measured by round-trip survival: source -> meta -> target -> meta -> source preserves meaning and source surface. | Implemented by `tests/unit/specification/translation_round_trip.rs`, which asserts both `MeaningId` equality and final surface equality. |\n| R526-2 | Translation to the meta language from every supported source language must be lossless for seeded surfaces. | Implemented by `supported_language_surfaces_survive_meta_language_round_trip`, covering `apple`, `яблоко`, `सेब`, and `苹果` through language-to-meta-to-same-language projection. |\n| R526-3 | Every supported natural-language pair must translate through the shared meta-language meaning rather than a direct pair-only path. | Implemented by `every_supported_language_pair_round_trips_via_meta_language`, which covers all directed pairs across en, ru, hi, and zh with `translate_via_default_pipeline`. |\n| R526-4 | Code translation must also preserve a meta-level meaning, including Rust <-> JavaScript for the reported cross-language code axis. | Implemented by `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs::rust_javascript_code_translation_round_trips_through_code_meaning` and `src/solver_helpers.rs::normalize_code_meaning`, which normalize the simple add-function slice to one `meaning:` link. |\n| R526-5 | The architecture must state that translation goes through the meta language and that direct translation bypasses are not the quality path. | Implemented in `VISION.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md` section 10, `ROADMAP.md`, and `CONTRIBUTING.md`. |\n| R526-6 | Issue data, online research, requirements, and solution planning must be compiled under `docs/case-studies/issue-526`. | Implemented by `docs/case-studies/issue-526/{README,requirements,solution-plans}.md`, `raw-data/online-research.md`, and raw GitHub snapshots. |\n\n## Issue #187 Current Day Calendar Prompt\n\nIssue [#187](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/187)",

every checked-in `.lino` file. |\n\n## Current Scope Boundary\n\nThis PR is a deterministic symbolic implementation, not a full universal problem solver. The implemented core proves that the repository can expose OpenAI-shaped interfaces while returning symbolic, inspectable, Links Notation-backed answers and reviewable seed datasets. Full imports of very large corpora such as Wikipedia, Wikidata, Rosetta Code, Wikifunctions, and hive-mind history should run as chunked follow-up dataset jobs behind the same `.lino` format and 1500-line file limit.\n\n## Issue #6 UI Follow-Up Requirements\n\nIssue [#6](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/6) adds these UI requirements to the demo surface:\n\n| ID | Requirement recovery. |\n| R47 | Log polling lifecycle events for operators. | Implemented through `eprintln!` lines that announce loop start, transport failures, invalid payloads, successful sends, send failures, and loop stop. |\n\n## Issue #10 Demo Feedback and Identity Requirements\n\nIssue [#10](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/10) adds feedback, issue reporting, and Covered by `Download bundle exports a formal_ai_bundle with seed and memory log` and `Report issue link is present in the topbar and prefills bundle hint`. |\n\n## Issue #16 Follow-Up: Universal Data/Seed Across Every Interface (PR #17 reopen)\n\nPR-17 review comment [4458370500](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/pull/17#issuecomment-4458370500)\nasks for `data/seed/` to be the single canonical knowledge surface for **every**\nuser-facing interface (Rust library, CLI, HTTP server, Telegram bot, browser\ndemo), for more behavior to move out of code constants and into `.lino` files,\nfor the merged bundle to be a true single-file import/export, and for the\nVISION / REQUIREMENTS / case-study documents to be kept in lockstep with the\nimplementation. The requirements below make full-memory\nexports the universal default on every surface and surface known data\nmigrations on import.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R109 | The default **Export memory** action must always produce the full, self-contained agent state (seed + memory log + UI preferences + environment metadata + version). No second button click is required to get the full export. | Implemented by `FormalAiMemory.exportFullMemory({ seed, events, preferences, info })` in `src/web/memory.js` (wired to the topbar `Export memory` button via `handleExportMemory` in `src/web/app.js`) and by the Rust mirror `memory::export_full_memory` in `src/memory.rs` (used by `formal-ai memory export` as the new default). |\n| R110 | Imports must accept both the legacy `demo_memory` document and the new `formal_ai_bundle` document so old exports continue to work. | Implemented by `FormalAiMemory.importFullMemory` (browser) and `memory::import_full_memory` (Rust). Both auto-detect the document header and fall back to the legacy `demo_memory` parser when no bundle header is present; covered by `import_full_memory_accepts_legacy_demo_memory` in `src/memory.rs`. |\n| R111 | After import, surface known data migrations (e.g. seed-version mismatch) directly in the UI so the user can take action without reading code. | Implemented by `FormalAiMemory.suggestMigrations` (browser) and `memory::suggest_migrations` (Rust). `handleImportMemory` in `src/web/app.js` appends the resulting suggestions to the memory-status indicator; the CLI `memory import` / `bundle import` commands print each suggestion via `eprintln!(\"Migration: <message>\")`. |\n| R112 | The prefilled \"Report issue\" body must instruct users to wrap the `.lino` export in a `.zip` (GitHub does not yet accept `.lino` attachments), and must explicitly remind users to redact sensitive content before attaching. | Implemented in `createIssueReportBody` in `src/web/app.js`: the body's **Attach full memory (optional)** line names `formal-ai-memory.lino`, references the `.zip` workaround and the redaction reminder inline, and links to [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) for the full walkthrough (issue #78 collapsed the previous three-paragraph block into one line + docs link so the encoded URL fits under GitHub's 8192-byte cap). |\n| R113 | The CLI surface (`formal-ai memory export`, `formal-ai bundle export`) and the Rust library API must default to full-memory output as well, so every interface stays consistent. | Implemented by switching `formal-ai memory export` to write the full `formal_ai_bundle` by default in `src/main.rs`; `--events-only` opts back into the legacy `demo_memory` shape. `formal_ai::export_memory_full`, `import_memory_full`, `suggest_memory_migrations`, `BundleInfo`, and `ParsedBundle` are re-exported from the crate root in `src/lib.rs`. |\n| R114 | Keep VISION.md, REQUIREMENTS.md, README.md, and the e2e/unit tests in lockstep with the new defaults so the documentation, the requirement matrix, and the regression coverage all describe the same behavior. | Implemented by this row plus the **Single-File Reproducibility** paragraph in `VISION.md`, the export/import notes in `README.md`, the rewritten `Export memory downloads a Links Notation file` Playwright e2e in `tests/e2e/tests/multilingual.spec.js` (now asserting `formal_ai_bundle` + `seed_files`), and the new `memory::tests::full_memory_round_trip_*` / `suggest_migrations_*` unit tests. |\n\n## Issue #78 Shorter Issue Reporting Requirements\n\nIssue [#78](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/78) reports\nthat the prefilled `Report issue` URL overflows GitHub's 8192-byte\n`/issues/new?body=…` limit (mobile shows *\"Whoops, something went wrong!\"*,\ndesktop shows *\"Whoa there! Your request URL is too long.\"*). The\nrequirements below shrink the prefilled body until it fits, by moving the\nlong memory-upload instructions into a single docs page and by recording\nthe dialog as a compact `U:` / `A:` code block.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R115 | The prefilled `Report issue` body must keep the encoded `body=` query parameter under GitHub's request-line limit so the new-issue page actually opens for typical sessions (no more *\"URL is too long\"*). | Implemented by collapsing the three-paragraph **Attach full memory** block in `createIssueReportBody` (`src/web/app.js`) into one sentence + docs link, and by switching the per-message subsection loop to a single fenced code block via `appendDialogBlock`. The compact form is exercised by `experiments/issue-78-dialog-format.mjs` and asserted indirectly by the e2e tests below. |\n| R116 | The dialog transcript embedded in the prefilled body must use a single fenced code block with `U:` / `A:` line prefixes, instead of one Markdown subsection per message, while still preserving the focused-message marker and the assistant `intent` annotation when the assistant could not classify the prompt. | Implemented by `appendDialogBlock` + `pickDialogFence` in `src/web/app.js`. The block opens with `Legend: \\`U\\` = user, \\`A\\` = agent.`; known-intent turns stay as plain `A: …` to match the issue example, and the focused-message marker plus the `intent` annotation only appear inline for the `unknown` intent (`A (intent: unknown, reported): …`) where they actually carry diagnostic value. Covered by the `known assistant dialogs include a prefilled issue report link` and `unknown prompts include a prefilled missing-rule issue link` tests in `tests/e2e/tests/demo.spec.js`. |\n| R117 | The verbose upload-memory instructions (Gist / `.zip` / per-OS walkthroughs / redaction reminders) must live in a single page checked into the repository so the prefilled body only needs a one-line pointer with a link. | Implemented by [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) and the matching link in the **Attach full memory (optional)** block in `createIssueReportBody`. The topbar `Report issue` and `Export memory` tooltips also point at the same doc instead of repeating the workflow. |\n| R118 | Documentation must explain that users can either upload the full memory export to a GitHub Gist (no extension restrictions) or wrap it in a `.zip` (GitHub's issue uploader still rejects raw `.lino`). | Implemented by the *Step 3 — Attach the file (choose one)* section of `docs/upload-memory.md`, which documents both Option A (Gist) and Option B (`.zip`) and links the GitHub attachment-types doc as the upstream constraint. |\n| R119 | The e2e regression suite must enforce all of the above: the prefilled body must contain `docs/upload-memory.md`, the dialog must use the `Legend: \\`U\\` = user, \\`A\\` = agent.` block, and the old verbose per-OS Compress / Send-to instructions must be absent. | Implemented by extending `Report issue link is present in the topbar and links to the upload-memory guide (R112 + issue #78)` in `tests/e2e/tests/multilingual.spec.js` (asserts `docs/upload-memory.md` and forbids the per-OS Compress / Send-to phrases) and by tightening the prefilled-body assertions in `tests/e2e/tests/demo.spec.js`. |\n\n## Issue #96 Calculator Delegation Requirements\n\nIssue [#96](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/96) asks formal-ai\nto use [`link-assistant/calculator`](https://github.com/link-assistant/calculator)\nas the delegated calculator backend for calculator-parsable expressions while\nkeeping local language processing and local fallbacks for unsupported syntax.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R120 | Add `link-calculator` as a library dependency and delegate calculator-parsable math, unit, currency, unknown-intent fallback. The maintainer\nasked for a real-time reasoning pipeline backed by Wikipedia/Wikidata/Wiktionary\ninstead of a hardcoded fact database. The seed entries are now treated as a\npre-warmed cache, and the JS worker reaches the live Wikidata API on cache\nmiss. Every step is recorded so memory holds the structured trace.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R173 | Multilingual factual prompts (capital, population, currency, official language, continent, area, head of state, head of government) must route to a typed `fact_query` intent instead of `unknown`. | Implemented by `parseFactQuestion` + `tryFactQuery` in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`, mirrored by `try_fact_lookup` in `src/solver_handlers/benchmark_prompts.rs`. |\n| R174 | The seed `data/seed/facts.lino` must declare structured `(relation, subject_qid, value_qid, subject_label, value_label)` triples for the pre-warmed countries. | Implemented for Russia, Japan, France, Germany, China, India, the United States, the United Kingdom, and Brazil, each with localized labels for en/ru/hi/zh. |\n| R175 | Recognized fact-query questions must be cached for one week with a per-language key, and the cache must be pre-warmed from the seed at worker startup. | Implemented by `factCachePut` / `factCacheGet` (1-week TTL) and `warmFactCacheFromSeed` in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`. |\n| R176 | The user must be able to force a fresh resolution by adding a fresh marker to the prompt in any supported language. | Implemented by `FORCE_FRESH_MARKERS` covering English, Russian, Hindi, and Chinese phrasings; recorded as a `fact_query:force_fresh` event. |\n| R177 | On cache miss the browser worker must resolve the prompt against the live Wikidata API (`wbsearchentities` + `wbgetentities`) using the property table `FACT_RELATIONS` and the user's language. | Implemented by `resolveFactQueryViaWikidata` in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`. |\n| R178 | Every step of the fact-query pipeline must be appended to memory as a `fact_query:*` event so the reasoning trace is reconstructible offline. | Implemented by the structured `fact_query:request`, `fact_query:relation`, `fact_query:subject`, `fact_query:cache:*`, `fact_query:wikidata:*`, and `fact_query:response` trace events emitted by the JS worker and (for the seed-cache path) the Rust solver. |\n| R179 | The Rust offline solver must emit the same `fact_query:*` evidence shape as the browser worker for seeded facts so memory inspection works uniformly across stacks. | Implemented by extending `try_fact_lookup` to emit `fact_query:relation`, `fact_query:subject`, `fact_query:cache:hit:seed`, `fact_query:subject_qid`, and `fact_query:value_qid` events; formatter branches added in `src/event_log.rs::build_evidence_links`. |\n| R180 | Tests must cover multiple phrasings per country and at least four supported languages so regressions in prompt parsing surface quickly. | Implemented by `CAPITAL_CASES` and the `capital_matrix_*` tests in `tests/unit/specification/prompt_variations.rs`, and by the parameterized `fact-query pipeline resolves` cases in `tests/e2e/tests/multilingual.spec.js`. |\n\n## Issue #133 DuckDuckGo Default, Combined Ranking, and Expanded Provider Diagnostics\n\nIssue [#133](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/133) asks for\nDuckDuckGo as the default search engine across every surface, a top-10\ncombined ranking that bubbles up URLs returned by more than one engine, an\nexpanded browser-only diagnostics matrix covering search engines, knowledge\ndatabases, code-hosting providers, and scientific paper providers, and full\nexportable memory for every reasoning step. The planner must run up to five\nproviders in parallel per category and temporarily disable providers that\nCORS-block.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R181 | DuckDuckGo must be the default search engine in CLI, server, and the browser-only GitHub Pages app. | Implemented by `WEB_SEARCH_PROVIDERS = &[\"duckduckgo\", \"wikipedia\", \"wikidata\"]` in `src/solver_handlers/web_requests.rs` and the matching `WEB_SEARCH_PROVIDERS` array in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`. |\n| R182 | The top-10 results from each available search engine must be combined into a single ranked list so URLs returned by more than one engine bubble up. | Implemented by `reciprocalRankFusion` in the JS worker (Cormack 2009, `k = 60`); each provider returns up to `WEB_SEARCH_PROVIDER_LIMIT = 10` results and the fused list is sorted by combined score with a provider-count tiebreaker. |\n| R183 | Both stacks must record the chosen reciprocal rank fusion constant in memory so the reasoning trace stays comparable offline. | Implemented by the `web_search:combined:rrf:k=60` event appended in both `src/solver_handlers/web_requests.rs` and `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`, with a matching formatter branch in `src/event_log.rs::build_evidence_links`. |\n| R184 | The browser-only diagnostics page must add more popular search engines beyond Google/Bing/DuckDuckGo/Brave/Yahoo. | Implemented by adding Yandex, Ecosia, Mojeek, and Startpage rows in `src/web/tests/connectivity.js`. |\n| R185 | The diagnostics page must add code-hosting providers including GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and providers from China and Russia. | Implemented by the new `code` category in `src/web/tests/connectivity.js` covering GitHub, GitLab, Codeberg, Gitee (China), Bitbucket Cloud, and GitFlic (Russia). |\n| R186 | The diagnostics page must add scientific paper providers without paywalls. | Implemented by the new `papers` category covering arXiv, Europe PMC, and DOAJ. |\n| R187 | The diagnostics page must add general knowledge providers beyond Wikipedia and Wikidata. | Implemented by adding Wiktionary and DBpedia Lookup to the `knowledge` category. |\n| R188 | Test cases must actually trigger external API access for reasoning instead of memoized snapshots. | Implemented by routing `tryWebSearch` through real `fetch()` calls against DuckDuckGo, Wikipedia REST, and Wikidata; the explicit `web_search` Playwright spec mocks the same endpoints with `page.route` so CI stays deterministic while exercising the real call path. |\n| R189 | Each reasoning step must be recorded in exportable memory so the full request, response, and unified-link interpretation can be replayed. | Implemented by the `web_search:request`, `web_search:provider`, `web_search:language`, `web_search:rank`, `web_search:fused`, `web_search:combined`, and `web_search:disabled` event kinds, with formatter branches in `src/event_log.rs::build_evidence_links`. |\n| R190 | The planner must run providers in parallel with a cap of five per category. | Implemented by `runWithConcurrencyLimit` (cap `WEB_SEARCH_CONCURRENCY = 5`) in the worker and by the matching `CATEGORY_CONCURRENCY = 5` runner in the dashboard. |\n| R191 | When a provider CORS-blocks or fails the network, the planner must temporarily disable it for the rest of the session and record the decision. | Implemented by the `WEB_SEARCH_DISABLED` map in the worker and the `state.disabled` map in the dashboard; both emit a `web_search:disabled:<provider>` / `disabled:<service-id>` entry and skip the provider until the page reloads. |\n| R192 | Issue data, online research, and case-study analysis must be compiled to `docs/case-studies/issue-133/`. | Implemented by `docs/case-studies/issue-133/README.md` and the raw-data folder (issue JSON, PR JSON, branch log, and online research). |\n| R193 | A changelog fragment must record the user-visible change and trigger an automated minor crate-version bump. | Implemented by `changelog.d/20260519_140000_issue_133_default_duckduckgo_rrf.md`, which declares `bump: minor` so the release pipeline raises the version from 0.69.0 on merge. |\n| R194 | As much logic as possible should be compiled from Rust to WebAssembly, with JavaScript reserved for UI. | Implemented boundary: `src/web_engine_core.rs` and `src/web_search_core.rs` own prompt normalization, language detection, arithmetic evaluation, stable FNV-1a ids, unknown-answer opener selection, intent-route matching semantics, provider order, RRF constants, request evidence, and fused ranking. `src/web/wasm-worker/src/lib.rs` exposes those primitives to the browser worker. JavaScript is intentionally retained for UI state, seed-file fetching/parsing, browser-only network calls/CORS handling, DOM integration, and no-WASM compatibility fallbacks; parity is covered by Rust unit tests, `experiments/issue-282-wasm-parity.mjs`, and the Issue #282 browser regression. |\n\n## Issue #159 Hive Mind Lookup and Curated Project Summarization\n\nIssue [#159](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/159) reported\nthat \"What is Hive Mind?\" / \"Что такое Hive Mind?\" prompts fell through to\nthe Wikipedia closest-match fallback and surfaced unrelated pages such as\nLOIC. The PR comments expanded the scope: deduplicate and simplify project\ndescriptions through a formalize → summarize → deformalize pipeline that\ndrops installation noise, keep purpose / language / stars / why-useful\nstatements, expose configurable lengths (`Topic` 1–5 words, `Short` ~20%,\n`Standard` ~50%, `Full` 100%, `Expand` ~200%), and use Natural Semantic\nMetalanguage primes plus compound-word substitution when the configuration\nrequests them. The maintainer\nreported three remaining problems with the prompt `Переведи \"как у тебя\nдела?\" на английский.`: the response body still looked like a robotic\n`meaning: … / surface (…): …` block, the translation discarded the\nsource fragment's lowercase casing (returning `How are you?` instead of\n`how are you?`), and only a single hardcoded meaning id resolved to a\nlocalized surface form — every other prompt fell back to a `[en] …`\nplaceholder. The maintainer asked for a single\n**formalize → meaning → deformalize** pipeline that handles every\nregistered meaning, preserves source-side formatting, and leaves room\nfor Wikipedia / Wikidata / Wiktionary enrichment.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R213 | Translation responses must read like natural conversation: the answer body is the deformalized target surface (quoted when the user quoted the source), not a multi-line `meaning: …` / `surface (…)` template. | Implemented by `try_translation` in `src/solver_handlers/mod.rs` and `tryTranslation` in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`. The meaning id, source language, and target language stay available through `evidence_links` so the trace remains inspectable. Covered by `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs::russian_translate_how_are_you_prompt_returns_english_surface`. |\n| R214 | Translations must preserve the source fragment's leading capitalization and terminal punctuation. A lowercase, unpunctuated source must produce a lowercase, unpunctuated target. | Implemented by `match_source_formatting` in `src/translation/formatting.rs` and `matchSourceFormatting` in `src/web/formal_ai_worker.js`. Covered by `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs::russian_translate_how_are_you_prompt_returns_english_surface`, `russian_capitalized_how_are_you_keeps_target_capitalization`, and `natural_translation_drops_terminal_when_source_has_none`. |\n| R215 | The pipeline must translate any surface, not only a hand-written subset, by routing through Wiktionary translation tables and Wikidata sense joins. Raw HTTP responses are cached on disk so unit tests run offline against real data. | Implemented by `TranslationPipeline` in `src/translation/pipeline.rs`, `Wiktionary` in `src/translation/wiktionary.rs`, `Wikidata` in `src/translation/wikidata.rs`, and `CachedHttpClient` in `src/translation/cache.rs`. Cached responses live under `data/translation-cache/`; integration runs can refresh them with `FORMAL_AI_LIVE_API=1`. Covered by `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs::translation_meaning_registry_covers_extended_phrases` (eight unrelated pairs across en / ru / hi / zh) plus per-module unit tests. The online enrichment design is documented in `ARCHITECTURE.md` section 10 and `docs/case-studies/issue-207/raw-data/online-research.md`. |\n\n## Issue #526 Translation Quality Test\n\nIssue [#526](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/526)\ndefines the translation quality rule as round-trip survival. A translation is\nnot sufficient merely because it renders a plausible target string; it must\nformalize into the meta language, deformalize into the target language, and\nreturn through the same meta-language meaning to the original source surface.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R526-1 | Translation quality must be measured by round-trip survival: source -> meta -> equality. |\n| R526-2 | Translation to the meta language from every supported source language must be lossless for seeded surfaces. | Implemented by `supported_language_surfaces_survive_meta_language_round_trip`, covering `apple`, `яблоко`, `सेब`, and `苹果` through language-to-meta-to-same-language projection. |\n| R526-3 | Every supported natural-language pair must translate through the shared meta-language meaning rather than a direct pair-only path. | Implemented by `every_supported_language_pair_round_trips_via_meta_language`, which covers all directed pairs across en, ru, hi, and zh with `translate_via_default_pipeline`. |\n| R526-4 | Code translation must also preserve a meta-level meaning, including Rust <-> JavaScript for the reported cross-language code axis. | Implemented by `tests/unit/specification/translation_via_links.rs::rust_javascript_code_translation_round_trips_through_code_meaning` and `src/solver_helpers.rs::normalize_code_meaning`, which normalize the simple add-function slice to one `meaning:` link. |\n| R526-5 | The architecture must state that translation goes through the meta language and that direct translation bypasses are not the quality path. | Implemented in `VISION.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md` section 10, `ROADMAP.md`, and `CONTRIBUTING.md`. |\n| R526-6 | Issue data, the browser e2e asserts they do not appear in rendered answers and checks semantic forbidden evidence for legacy routes. |\n| R249 | WASM must remain the bridge for shared primitives while JS performs browser-only composition and glue. | Browser synthesis delegates arithmetic evaluation and stable id creation through `wasmEvaluateArithmetic` and `wasmStableId` when available, with JS fallbacks only for no-WASM compatibility. |\n\n## Issue #244 Vision Implementation Planning\n\nIssue [#244](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/244) is a\nmeta-planning issue: before any new feature code, it requires the documentation\nto **fully track implementation progress** and stay **in sync with the actual\nstate of the code**, and then for the GitHub issues that fully implement the\nvision to be created from a deep case study (with online research, every\nrequirement listed, and a solution plan per requirement that checks existing\ncomponents). The deliverables of this requirement are documentation and planning\nartifacts, not feature code; the feature work itself is tracked by the created\nplanning issues. The first foundation batch (E1-E14, [#246](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/246)-[#259](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/259))\nand the second follow-up batch (E15-E20, [#278](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/278)-[#283](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/283))\nare all closed and merged. A third audit (2026-05-26) re-read the closed issues\nagainst the vision and opened the reasoning batch E21-E27 ([#298](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/298)-[#304](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/304)):\nreasoning under unknowns, intent formalization in Links Notation, generalized\nparametric intents, substitution-rule (`replace`/`when … do …`) handlers over\nlink CRUD, natural-language access to memory/APIs/code execution, a general\ncode-modifying/executing agent, and industry benchmark datasets — **now all\nclosed and merged** (PRs #305-#311). A fourth audit (2026-05-27) found the\nremaining gap was the **generality of the synthesis step** and opened the\nsynthesis batch E28-E32 ([#313](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/313)-[#317](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/317)):\na general link-native synthesis substrate, computed math/word-problem and\ncounting answers, general program synthesis from spec+tests, general text\nmanipulation, and a grown/ratcheted benchmark suite — **now all closed and\nmerged** (PRs #319-#323), with the benchmark suite passing 10/10. A fifth audit\n(2026-05-29, PR #245 feedback) found the remaining gap is **parity** and opened\nthe parity batch E33-E34 ([#326](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/326)-[#327](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/327)):\na single shared, data-driven multilingual operation vocabulary so every handler\ntriggers equally in `en|ru|hi|zh`, and a cross-runtime sweep so the JavaScript\nbrowser worker derives the same answers as the Rust core — **now all closed and\nmerged** (PRs #328-#329). With E1-E34 all merged, no vision-planning epic remains\nopen for issue #244.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R250 | Documentation must fully track the implementation progress Meta-Language\n\nIssue [#398](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/398) requires\nthe meaning seed to move from English-only descriptions toward a recursive\nsemantic meta-language where meanings describe meanings, source evidence is\ncached and inspectable, and notation, annotation, denotation, and connotation are\nrepresented as meaning links. This PR implements the seed/parser foundation and\nrecords the larger source-import/backfill plan in the case study.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R266 | Issue #398 research, issue metadata, PR metadata, comments, reviews, and online source analysis must be preserved under `docs/case-studies/issue-398`. | Implemented with `docs/case-studies/issue-398/README.md` and `raw-data/*`, including the online source survey for Wikidata, Wiktionary/Wikipedia dumps, WordNet, SKOS, and OntoLex-Lemon. |\n| R267 | Meanings must remain recursively describable by other meanings instead of relying only on prose descriptions. | Preserved and extended by the existing `defined_by` closure/root-reachability tests plus the new meaning-to-meaning semantic facet parser. |\n| R268 | Notation, annotation, denotation, and connotation must be represented as seed meanings, not hardcoded Rust vocabulary. | Implemented by `data/seed/meanings-semantic-meta.lino`; the parser recognizes only the generic `facet` container and resolves facet kind/target strings through the lexicon. |\n| R269 | The root `link` meaning must declare the required semantic facets as meaning references. | Implemented in `data/seed/meanings-ontology.lino` and verified by `root_link_declares_the_required_semantic_facets`. |\n| R270 | Semantic facet blocks must parse as meaning references and resolve through the lexicon. | Implemented by `SemanticFacet`, `Meaning::semantic_facet_targets`, and `Lexicon::semantic_facet_meanings`; verified by `semantic_facet_blocks_are_parsed_as_meaning_references`. |\n| R271 | External knowledge sources and cached source responses must become first-class semantic concepts so future imports can preserve provenance. | Seeded as `external_knowledge_source` and `cached_source_response` meanings; full source-response importers are tracked as follow-up work in the case study. |\n| R272 | The semantic meta-language must stay small at startup while allowing on-demand expansion from external corpora. | Implemented by adding only the compact facet vocabulary to `data/seed/`; the case study recommends chunked `.lino` source-response caches for large corpora. |\n| R273 | Vision documentation must reflect recursive meaning descriptions and semantic facets. | Implemented in `VISION.md` under \"Meaning And Identity\". |\n| R274 | User-facing changes must be captured for release notes. | Implemented by `changelog.d/20260606_201500_issue_398_semantic_facets.md`. |\n| R275 | The self-defining Links-Theory root draft from PR feedback must be executable seed data, not only prose in a comment. | Implemented by `data/seed/meanings-links-root.lino`, embedded in `src/seed/embedded.rs` and `tests/source/seed/embedded.rs`, with `semantic_root` tests for root terms and closure. |\n| R276 | Self-referential primitives must be represented as meaning-backed self-equations. | Implemented by the `self_equation` semantic facet kind plus `type`, `not`, and `same` facet links, covered by `self_equations_are_explicit_semantic_facets`. |\n| R277 | Ambiguous symbols must split into one-symbol-one-meaning records. | Implemented by `one_symbol_one_meaning`, `sense_split`, `bank_river`, and `bank_money`; `ambiguous_bank_surface_is_split_into_distinct_symbols` asserts there is no ambiguous bare `bank` meaning. |\n\n## Issue #398 PR Review Standards (comment 4663407299)\n\nThe PR [#399](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/pull/399) review of\n2026-06-09 added data-quality standards for the meaning seed and its external\ngrounding. These standards govern the whole `data/` tree (and `src/`), are\napplied through re-runnable migrations rather than manual edits, and each is\nlocked by a directory-walking CI check that names no individual file.\n\n**Governance — latest requirement overrides any earlier one.** When two\nrequirements in this document conflict, the one with the higher `R` number\nwins; the earlier requirement is superseded for the overlapping scope. Every CI\ncheck listed below maps to exactly one requirement so a reviewer can trace a red\nbuild back to the rule it protects.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R278 | Semantic facets must use the native Links Notation `subject predicate` form (`notation word_surface`); empty-bodied colon redefinitions (`word_surface:`, `concept:` with no value) are banned across the whole `data/seed` tree. | Implemented by `scripts/migrate-empty-facet-fields.rs` (tree-wide collapse), the dual-form `parse_semantic_facets` in `src/seed/meanings.rs`, and the tree-walking CI guard `seed_lino_files_have_no_empty_redefinition_fields` in `tests/unit/data_files.rs`. |\n| R279 | A `data/overrides/` layer must sit beside `data/cache/` with the same per-id structure; resolution is `(cache or live API) then overrides`; every override records why it exists; CI must fail when an override is redundant (the cache already carries its value), forcing removal. | Implemented by `data/overrides/` (mirroring `data/cache/`), `data/overrides/README.md`, `formal_ai::seed::resolve` in `src/seed/grounding_overrides.rs`, and the tree-walking CI suite `overrides_are_disciplined_and_non_redundant` / `overrides_layer_mirrors_the_cache_directory_structure` in `tests/unit/overrides.rs`. |\n| R280 | Every seed/grounding data-quality rule must be enforced by a directory-walking CI check with no hard-coded filename, so a new file cannot bypass a rule. | Implemented; R278 and R279 guards both walk their whole trees (`seed_lino_paths()`, `WalkDir` over `data/overrides`) and assert on relative paths rather than fixed names. |\n| R281 | Tree-wide fixes must be applied through re-runnable migration/refresh scripts, not one-off manual edits, so the transform is auditable and idempotent. | Implemented by `scripts/migrate-empty-facet-fields.rs` and `scripts/clean-seed-readability.rs`, both std-only and lossless, which also regenerate the embedded browser worker fallback. |\n| R282 | Meanings must be grounded in real external sources (Wikidata / Wiktionary / WordNet); per-language surfaces must come from those sources rather than being hand-typed, and the grounding closure must verify external evidence, not only local `defined-by` resolution. | Partially implemented: source-grounded meanings carry `grounded-in <Qid>` links whose recursive closure is verified against checked-in cache records by `wikidata_cache_records_cover_recursive_grounding_closure` and `seed_and_source_wikidata_ids_have_checked_in_cache_records`. Expanding grounding from the current core set to every meaning is tracked as ongoing source-import work in `docs/case-studies/issue-398/README.md`; the override layer (R279) is the mechanism for recording source gaps in the meantime. |\n| R283 | The standards introduced by this review must be recorded in `REQUIREMENTS.md` under the latest-overrides-earlier governance, with each CI check mapped to a requirement. | Implemented by this section; the mapping column names the enforcing test for R278-R281. |\n\n## Issue #398 PR Review Standards (comment 4668929105)\n\nThe 2026-06-10 review accepted the corrected count (478 defined meanings, two\ndefinition syntaxes) and the backbone `reference_closure.rs` gate, but required\nthat closure be widened from the structured backbone to **every** value token,\nand that the agreed multi-source `view` infrastructure be built and\nCI-enforced. [#451](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/451) asks the\nproject to reference the Wikipedia *Symbolic artificial intelligence* article in\nthe docs, adopt the field's best known per-requirement\nsolution plans (surveying existing components). | The single audit gap (§3.2 SAT / constraint solving), previously *proposed*, must be closed by shipping a real decision procedure rather than left deferred, generalizing the propositional path while keeping every prior test green. | Implemented by the in-house deterministic DPLL backend `src/proof_engine/decision/sat.rs` (`CnfFormula`/`Literal`/`SatOutcome`) wired into `src/proof_engine/decision/boolean.rs`: claims wider than the eight-variable truth-table limit are Tseitin-encoded and solved in-process (UNSAT ⇒ tautology proof, SAT ⇒ countermodel disproof). The `splr`/`varisat` crates stay cited as evaluated reuse alternatives, set aside to keep the engine dependency-free and WebAssembly-safe. Covered by `tests/source/source_tests/proof_engine/decision/{sat,boolean}/tests.rs` and the `proof_request.rs` integration cases. |\n\n## Issue #468 Agentic-Coding Mode\n\nIssue [#468](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/468) — title\n*\"Our system should be able to solve such tasks in agentic mode\"* — is reframed by\nthe maintainer comment: the text-formalization protocol in the issue body (Igor\nMartynov's *Formal protocol for translating texts into a knowledge base*, with\n«Сказка о рыбаке и рыбке» as the canonical input) is **an example task** the system\nshould solve from the prompt, and the **core task** is the agentic capability —\n*\"our Formal AI is able to solve the task in agentic coding mode … call all the\ntools from any agentic CLI, understand errors from tools … web fetch and web\nsearch\"*. Per the comment the project must also collect the issue data, produce a\ndeep case study with online research, enumerate every requirement, and propose\nper-requirement solution plans surveying existing components. Two constraints bind:\nthe maintainer's *everything is a link* position means the example task's knowledge\nbase is emitted directly in Links Notation (the meta-language already in the code\nbase), not as typed-struct entities/ontologies; and external CLIs (`claude`,\n`codex`) are pointed *at* the server, never wired *into* the Formal AI engine. The\nwork lands in PR [#469](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/pull/469).\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R306 | The application must provide a capability to solve the example task — translating texts into a knowledge base following the formal protocol, with «Сказка о рыбаке и рыбке» as the canonical input. | Implemented by `formalize_text_to_links` (`src/agentic_coding/formalize.rs`), which drives the example task end to end; surfaced through `formal-ai agent` (`src/main.rs`) and re-exported from `src/agentic_coding/mod.rs`. |\n| R307 | The nine protocol primitives must be realized: Concept, Entity, Predicate, Assertion, Procedure, Context, Temporal, Modal, Annotation (the article's full field set is the spec). | Implemented as the nine Links Notation record kinds `PRIMITIVE_KINDS` (`src/agentic_coding/formalize.rs`), each emitted as a record by the formalizer; for the canonical synopsis `summary.covers_all_nine()` is `true`, pinned in `tests/unit/agentic_coding.rs`. The formalizer populates the deterministically-extractable field subset; the full article field set is captured in `docs/case-studies/issue-468/raw-data/article-summary.md`. |\n| R308 | The representation must be assertion-centric — assertions are the units of search, inference, and aggregation. | Implemented: each fact is one atomic `assertion` record whose `subject`/`predicate`/`object` associations are links into the entity/predicate/concept catalogue and whose qualifiers are ordinary associations; search/inference/aggregation run over that emitted Links Notation store with the crate's general links tooling rather than a bespoke query engine. |\n| R309 | The operational format must stay distinct from an ontology schema: assertions + procedures + modality + provenance carry the facts, while Concept/Predicate declarations remain a fact-free reference directory. | Implemented by a structural `source` vs `provenance` distinction: text-derived assertions carry `provenance \"<doc>@<start>:<end>\"`, while lexicon-sourced catalogue records (concept/procedure/context) are tagged `source \"lexicon:<doc>\"`, keeping the reference directory separable from the facts without a schema layer. |\n| R310 | Each assertion must carry the protocol's qualifiers: modality, time, context, confidence, and provenance. | Implemented: `assertion` records carry optional `time`/`modal`/`context` associations plus `annotation` + `provenance`; the `temporal` and `modal` primitives (the latter with a `degree`) are emitted as their own links and referenced by id, mapped in `docs/case-studies/issue-468/formal-protocol-mapping.md` and pinned in `tests/unit/agentic_coding.rs`. |\n| R311 | The maintainer's position that everything is a link must be honored: the primitives are implemented as is, in the meta-language already in the code base. | Implemented: every primitive is emitted directly as a Links Notation record via `format_lino_record` (`src/links_format.rs`) — there is no typed-struct or protocol-JSON layer — so each primitive *is* a link; both the record count and `covers_all_nine()` are pinned in `tests/unit/agentic_coding.rs`. |\n| R313 | Deterministic extraction must be provided for the example, and general open-domain NL→KB extraction (which needs neural inference, a project NON-GOAL) must be scoped honestly rather than faked. | Implemented: every sentence becomes an `annotation` with real character offsets; the closed-class lexicon (`src/agentic_coding/lexicon.rs`, data in `data/agentic-coding/fisherman-lexicon.lino`) turns recognised subject-predicate-object triples into structured assertions, and unrecognised sentences become honest natural-language assertions that never guess; general extraction is scoped as future work in `docs/case-studies/issue-468/README.md` §4.6/§7. |\n| R314 | The title's \"agentic mode\" must be solved: the system must be driven by an agentic CLI against the OpenAI-compatible server — calling tools, understanding errors, web search/fetch, bash — while honoring the constraint not to wire external CLIs (claude/codex) into Formal AI. | **Implemented — the core task.** The deterministic planner `plan_chat_step` (`src/agentic_coding/planner.rs`) drives the `web_search → web_fetch → write_file → run_command → final` loop, wired into the shared `agentic_outcome` decision (`src/protocol.rs`) so all three OpenAI-shaped surfaces (`/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/messages`, `/v1/responses`) emit and consume tool calls identically; the in-repo `run_agentic_task` driver + offline corpus (`src/agentic_coding/driver.rs`, `corpus.rs`) exercise it offline; gated by `agent_mode` + `pkg_agentic_coding`. External CLIs are documented as front-ends *against* the server in `docs/desktop/server-api.md`, never embedded. Pinned in `tests/unit/agentic_coding.rs` and `tests/unit/agentic_surfaces.rs`. |\n| R315 | Issue-related data must be collected into `docs/case-studies/issue-468/`. | Implemented: `docs/case-studies/issue-468/raw-data/` holds `issue-468.json`, `issue-468-comments.json`, `pr-469.json`, `article-summary.md`, and `online-research.md`. |\n| R316 | A deep case-study analysis must be produced, including online research for additional facts and data. | Implemented by `docs/case-studies/issue-468/README.md` §4 and `docs/case-studies/issue-468/raw-data/online-research.md` (8 cited sources: AMR, OpenIE, RDF reification/RDF-star, Wikidata qualifiers, FrameNet/PropBank, the NL→KB pipeline, the associative model, and the Tale provenance). |\n| R317 | Each and all requirements from the issue must be listed. | Implemented: requirements R306–R319 enumerated here and in `docs/case-studies/issue-468/README.md` §3. |\n| R318 | Possible solutions and solution plans must be proposed per requirement, checking existing components/libraries. | Implemented by `docs/case-studies/issue-468/README.md` §6 (per-requirement plans) and §7 (prior-art survey: Wikidata qualifiers, FrameNet/PropBank/VerbNet, `doublets-rs`, Neo4j). |\n| R319 | Everything must be planned and executed in the single PR #469. | Implemented: the `src/agentic_coding/` module, the `tests/unit/docker_runtime.rs::compose_file_offers_optional_openai_compatible_server_profile`. |\n\n## Issue #559 General Meta Algorithm\n\nIssue [#559](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/559) asks to\ngeneralize the solver away from hardcoded specific intents toward a single\ngeneral meta algorithm: translate every message into the meta language, detect\nall questions/requirements/needs, address each in the answer, and let the core\nreason about its own method set and review-gated improvements. PR\n[#560](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/pull/560) lands the live\nregistry-backed meta method path plus the link-native audit artifacts. The full\nplan, requirements derivation, recursive core, and option comparison are in\n`docs/case-studies/issue-559/`. New rows\nR330–R344 capture obligations genuinely new to this issue; most of #559 is\nrealized through existing rows (R72/R74/R97/R103/R157/R158/R67/R264/R311/R314/R129).\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R330 | Every prompt must produce an explicit, link-serializable problem frame — the meaning record made first-class — emitted as a solver loop event and enumerating every detected need. | Implemented by `src/meta_frame.rs` (`ProblemFrame`, `Need`, `record_problem_frame`), wired into `src/solver.rs::solve_with_history_probability_store_and_intent_cache` as the trace-only `problem_frame` event, serialized via `src/links_format.rs::format_lino_record`, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/meta_frame.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_problem_frame_is_traceable`. |\n| R331 | The catalogue of methods each atomic work-unit leaf can route to must exist as first-class link data and as the live method-selection authority: a method registry derived from the live dispatch code, including prelude methods, specialized handlers, and contextual overrides, must serialize to Links Notation, emit as a loop event, and drive the solver's single meta method dispatch path. | Implemented by `src/method_registry.rs` (`MethodRegistry`, `Method`, `MethodSurface`, `MethodRegistry::from_dispatch`, `ordered_method_names_for_relevants`, `record_method_registry`) built from `src/solver_dispatch.rs` (`PRELUDE_METHOD_NAMES`, `SPECIALIZED_HANDLERS`, `CONTEXTUAL_HANDLER_NAMES`, `handler_for_method`) and executed by `src/meta_method_dispatch.rs::try_dispatch`; wired into `src/solver.rs::solve_with_history_probability_store_and_intent_cache` as the live registry-backed path and the `method_registry` event, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/method_registry.rs`, `tests/unit/specification/reasoning_paths.rs::selected_specialized_handler_is_recorded_as_a_meta_method`, and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_method_registry_is_traceable`. |\n| R332 | The core must decompose every frame as a recursive, bounded work-unit tree (downward pass): each unit either maps to a direct method, is an irreducible single need, or is split into children, stopping at `max_decomposition_depth`; the tree is link-serializable and emitted as loop events without changing routing or the answer. | Implemented by `src/meta_frame.rs` (`WorkUnit`, `AtomicityReason`, `decompose_once`, `record_work_units`), wired into `src/solver.rs::solve_with_history_probability_store_and_intent_cache` as the trace-only `work_unit` / `work_unit:enter` / `work_unit:exit` events bounded by `SolverConfig::max_decomposition_depth`, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/meta_frame.rs` and `tests/unit/specification/reasoning_loop.rs::handler_families_publish_loop_events_as_recursion_leaves`. |\n| R333 | Every detected need must carry an explicit status in a need-satisfaction ledger so \"address every detected need\" is structural, not prose: a need that maps to a known method is satisfiable, a need with no recognized method is recorded as blocked rather than silently dropped, and the ledger is link-serializable and emitted as a loop event. | Implemented by `src/meta_frame.rs` (`NeedLedger`, `LedgerRow`, `NeedLedger::resolve`, `every_need_accounted_for`, `record_need_ledger`), wired into `src/solver.rs::solve_with_history_probability_store_and_intent_cache` as the trace-only `need_ledger` / `need:status` events, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/meta_frame.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_need_ledger_is_traceable`. |\n| R334 | The core must join its separate link artifacts (problem frame, work-unit tree, need ledger, method registry) into one end-to-end evidence chain ��� per need, `frame need → work-unit leaf → status → catalogued method` — so \"ensure every detected need is addressed\" is a single auditable record; the evidence is link-serializable and emitted as a loop event without changing routing or the answer. | Implemented by `src/solution_evidence.rs` (`SolutionEvidence`, `EvidenceTrail`, `SolutionEvidence::assemble`, `accounted_for`, `fully_resolved`, `record_solution_evidence`), enabled by additive `unit_id`/`route` links on `src/meta_frame.rs::LedgerRow`, wired into `src/solver.rs::solve_with_history_probability_store_and_intent_cache` as the trace-only `solution_evidence` event, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/solution_evidence.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_solution_evidence_is_traceable`. |\n| R335 | The meta algorithm must describe itself as grounded link data — a recipe enumerating the ordered steps that turn any message into a solved knowledge base, each pinned to the live function that implements it — so the core's own algorithm is data the engine can read rather than prose that drifts. | Implemented by `data/meta/recursive-core-recipe.lino` (the twelve-step `meta_recipe` plus `meta_function` records citing `formalize_intent`, `record_problem_frame`, `decompose_once`, `record_work_units`, `NeedLedger::resolve`, `record_need_ledger`, `MethodRegistry::from_dispatch`, `record_method_registry`, `MethodRegistry::method_for_route`, `method_for_alias`, `WorkUnitReasoning::for_unit`, `record_work_unit_reasoning`, `UpwardConstruction::for_unit`, `record_upward_construction`, `try_contextual_override`, `try_dispatch`, `MethodSelection::for_unit`, `record_selection`, `record_solution_evidence`, `SkillLedger::from_evidence`, `record_skill_ledger`, `build_evidence_links`, `RecipeProgram::from_lino`, and `RecipeProgram::execute`), kept grounded by `tests/unit/specification/recursive_core_recipe.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_recursive_core_recipe_is_traceable`. |\n| R336 | The bridge between the meta-language route vocabulary (intent slugs on work-unit leaves) and the method vocabulary (handler names in the registry) must exist as first-class link data, so a route slug coarser or finer than the handler that serves it (for example `write_program` → `write_script`) still resolves to a catalogued method in both solution evidence and live registry-backed dispatch; the map must be grounded (every alias target is a real method, every alias is necessary). | Implemented by `data/meta/route-method-aliases.lino` (the `route_method_alias` records) and `src/route_method_alias.rs` (`RouteMethodAlias`, `aliases`, `method_for_alias`), consumed by `src/method_registry.rs::MethodRegistry::method_for_route` and surfaced in `src/solution_evidence.rs` (the `method`/`method_via_alias` links on `EvidenceTrail`) plus `src/meta_method_dispatch.rs` method ordering, kept grounded by `tests/unit/specification/route_method_alias.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_route_method_alias_is_traceable`. |\n| R337 | Each recursive step of the meta core must carry white-box recursive reasoning — a human-readable thought attached to every work unit, mirroring the recursion in both directions: the downward thought (what was observed, why the unit was decomposed or judged atomic, and which method an atomic leaf resolves to) and the upward thought (how the unit's answer is composed from its solved children) — so the box is inspectable by users and developers, not just the predicate. The reasoning is a parallel tree to the work-unit tree, link-serializable, emitted as a loop event, and trace-only: it changes neither routing nor the answer. | Implemented by `src/meta_reasoning.rs` (`WorkUnitReasoning`, `WorkUnitReasoning::for_unit`, `to_links_notation`, `record_work_unit_reasoning`), resolving leaf methods through `src/method_registry.rs::MethodRegistry::method_for_route`, wired into `src/meta_core.rs::record_meta_core` as the trace-only `work_unit_reasoning` / `work_unit_reasoning:steps` events, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/meta_reasoning.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_work_unit_reasoning_is_traceable`. |\n| R338 | The meta core's recursion must be bidirectional and the emitted direction(s) must be a configurable knob: besides the downward decomposition reasoning (R337), the core must record an explicit upward construction pass — a post-order (leaf→root) walk that constructs each leaf's answer from the method that resolves it (the base case) and each parent's answer by composing its already-constructed children in source order (the recursive case) — link-serialized and emitted as a loop event. A `recursion_mode` knob selects which directions are traced (`down` default, `up`, `both`); `down` reproduces the pre-knob trace exactly. The structural work-unit decomposition events stay always-on regardless of the knob, and the whole pass is trace-only: it changes neither routing nor the answer. | Implemented by `src/meta_construction.rs` (`RecursionMode`, `emits_downward`, `emits_upward`, `from_slug`, `UpwardConstruction`, `ConstructionStep`, `UpwardConstruction::for_unit`, `to_links_notation`, `record_upward_construction`), resolving leaf methods through `src/method_registry.rs::MethodRegistry::method_for_route`, gated by `SolverConfig::recursion_mode` and wired into `src/meta_core.rs::record_meta_core` as the trace-only `upward_construction` / `upward_construction:steps` events, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/meta_construction.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_upward_construction_is_traceable`. |\n| R339 | The meta core must record a method-selection trace from the single data-driven authority — the registry resolver. For every atomic work-unit leaf it must name the method the registry resolves (`MethodRegistry::method_for_route`, alias-aware so e.g. `write_program` resolves to `write_script`) or mark the leaf `unresolved`, and partition the leaves into resolved and unresolved counts. The legacy hardcoded route mapper was removed entirely once the registry became the sole dispatch authority, so the artifact records what the one authority selects rather than a comparison against a retired baseline. A `selection_mode` knob selects what is recorded (`off` default records nothing, `record` emits the per-leaf selection); this knob controls trace verbosity only — it changes neither routing nor the answer (R13). | Implemented by `src/selection.rs` (`SelectionMode`, `emits_artifact`, `slug`, `from_slug`, `LeafSelection`, `MethodSelection`, `MethodSelection::for_unit`, `leaf_count`, `resolved_count`, `unresolved_count`, `to_links_notation`, `record_selection`), resolving each leaf through `src/method_registry.rs::MethodRegistry::method_for_route`, gated by `SolverConfig::selection_mode` and wired into `src/meta_core.rs::record_meta_core` as the optional `selection` event, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/selection.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_selection_trace_is_traceable`. |\n| R340 | The meta algorithm must be able to reason about improving itself: it must take itself — the recursive-core recipe, the algorithm encoded as Links Notation — together with what it is required to do (the stages the live `record_meta_core` pipeline runs), both meta-language encoded, and emit the updated algorithm as link-encoded output. The loop must detect drift between the algorithm-as-data (the recipe's `meta_function` citations) and the algorithm-as-code (the `record_*` stages the pipeline invokes), proposing the recipe additions and stale-citation removals that reconcile them. It must be gated and proposal-only: the default `off` mode proposes nothing and it never writes the recipe back — adoption stays a human review step (mirroring R12/C3) — so it changes neither routing nor the answer. | Implemented by `src/meta_self_improvement.rs` (`SelfImprovementMode`, `proposes`, `from_slug`, `PipelineStage`, `MetaRecipeProposal`, `is_self_consistent`, `change_count`, `to_links_notation`, `MetaSelfImprovement`, `MetaSelfImprovement::from_sources`, `MetaSelfImprovement::from_repo`, `propose`, `propose_recipe_update`), reading `data/meta/recursive-core-recipe.lino` against `src/meta_core.rs`, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/meta_self_improvement.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_meta_self_improvement_is_traceable`. |\n| R341 | The hardcoded natural-language cue lists that drive the meta core's intent recognition must move out of inline Rust literals into first-class, reviewable link data: each cue belongs to a named cue set declaring how it is matched (`token` whitespace-bounded word / CJK substring, `substring` raw contains, or `prefix` starts-with), and the Rust call sites read the cue strings from that data while keeping only the structural glue (digit presence, AND/OR composition, which input each set is tested against). The data must be grounded — every cue set the code consults exists with the expected mode, and the migrated cue contents reproduce exactly the lists they replaced — so the migration is behavior-preserving: it changes neither routing nor the answer (R13), and a new trigger word becomes a data edit rather than a Rust change. | Implemented by `data/meta/cue-lexicon.lino` (the `cue_set` records) and `src/cue_lexicon.rs` (`CueMatch`, `CueSet`, `cue_sets`, `cue_set`, `matches`, `cues`), consumed by `src/intent_formalization.rs` (`append_prompt_relevants`, `looks_arithmetic`, `looks_like_text_manipulation`), kept grounded by `tests/unit/specification/cue_lexicon.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_cue_lexicon_is_traceable`. |\n| R342 | The meta core must turn each request's outcome into accumulated learning the next request can reuse — the deterministic analog of an agent that grows a skill library and a curriculum: every detected need that was satisfied by a catalogued method becomes a proposed, reusable candidate skill (a named capability the solver demonstrably has, captured with the span that demonstrated it), and every blocked need becomes a curriculum item recording the gap to close rather than a silent failure. Accumulation must be proposal-only and gated: a candidate skill is born `proposed` and may only be promoted to `stable` once its promotion gate is satisfied — tests *and* a benchmark delta — so the promotable count is always zero at trace time and no skill is ever auto-promoted without review (C3); the default `off` mode records nothing, so the ledger changes neither routing nor the answer (R13). | Implemented by `src/skill_ledger.rs` (`SkillMode`, `SkillStatus`, `PromotionGate`, `satisfied`, `CandidateSkill`, `promotable`, `CurriculumItem`, `SkillLedger`, `SkillLedger::from_evidence`, `proposed_count`, `stable_count`, `curriculum_count`, `promotable_count`, `record_skill_ledger`), distilling `src/solution_evidence.rs::SolutionEvidence`, gated by `SolverConfig::skill_mode` (env `FORMAL_AI_SKILL_MODE`) and wired into `src/meta_core.rs::record_meta_core` as the trace-only `skill_ledger` / `skill_ledger:promotable` events, described as the twelfth step of `data/meta/recursive-core-recipe.lino`, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/skill_ledger.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_skill_ledger_is_traceable`. |\n| R343 | The recursive-core recipe must not only *describe* the meta algorithm as link data (R335) but be *executable* as that data: each trace-recorded step in the recipe must bind to the recorder primitive it drives (a `records` field, e.g. `record_problem_frame`), and an interpreter must parse the recipe into an ordered program and run it — invoking those primitives in the order the data declares, threading the intermediate artifacts exactly as the hand-written pipeline does, and mirroring the pipeline's mode gates. The proof obligation is parity: executing the recipe must reproduce, event-for-event, the event log that `meta_core::record_meta_core` produces for the same input across every mode combination, and the recipe's recorder order must equal the live pipeline's actual stage order. A misordered or unknown binding must surface as an error rather than silent divergence; execution stays trace-only, changing neither routing nor the answer (R13). | Implemented by `src/recipe_interpreter.rs` (`RecipeStep`, `RecipeProgram`, `RecipeProgram::from_lino`, `from_repo`, `recorder_sequence`, `execute`, `reproduces_pipeline`, `to_links_notation`, `ExecutionTrace`) driving the recorder primitives in `src/meta_frame.rs`, `src/method_registry.rs`, `src/meta_reasoning.rs`, `src/meta_construction.rs`, `src/solution_evidence.rs`, `src/selection.rs`, and `src/skill_ledger.rs`, executing the `records`-annotated steps of `data/meta/recursive-core-recipe.lino` against the same `src/meta_core.rs::record_meta_core` it reproduces, and verified by `tests/unit/specification/recipe_interpreter.rs` and `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_recipe_interpreter_is_traceable`. |\n| R344 | The migration to the data-driven registry as the **sole dispatch authority** must be total: once the corpus-wide parity certificate proved the registry resolved the *entire route vocabulary the system can ever emit* as a behavior-preserving replacement, the legacy hardcoded route mapper and its audit scaffolding must be removed outright — no obsolete second authority kept \"as a baseline\". The closure invariant the certificate guaranteed must survive directly against the live registry: enumerated from live data — every registered method name (a method is its own self-resolving route), every route→method alias (R336, which includes the `write_program` intent), and every classifier route slug — no route may resolve to an *unregistered* method, and every method-name and alias route must resolve. | Implemented by removing `src/dispatch_parity.rs` and `src/intent_formalization.rs::specialized_handler_name` entirely (the legacy authority and its parity audit), leaving `src/method_registry.rs::MethodRegistry::method_for_route` (alias-aware via `src/route_method_alias.rs`) as the only route→method resolver and live dispatch path (`src/meta_method_dispatch.rs`); the corpus closure invariant — grounded in `MethodRegistry::from_dispatch`, `route_method_alias::aliases`, and `seed::intent_routing` — is verified by `tests/unit/specification/method_registry.rs::the_registry_is_the_sole_authority_that_closes_over_the_route_corpus`, and the full retirement by `tests/unit/docs_requirements_issue_559.rs::issue_559_legacy_dispatch_authority_is_fully_retired`. |\n\n## Issue #563 Repository Resource Summarization\n\nIssue [#563](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/563) asks the\nsummarization pipeline to handle arbitrary repository files, formalize each file\nbefore summarizing it, recurse into Markdown embedded grammars, and preserve the\nissue research under `docs/case-studies/issue-563`. PR\n[#564](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/pull/564) adds a repository\nfile formalization boundary on top of the existing deterministic\n`formalize -> summarize -> deformalize` pipeline. New rows R345-R354 capture\nthe issue-specific obligations.\n\nReview feedback on PR #564 (konard) made the generalization explicit: the\nsolution must not stay file-specialized, but follow the meta algorithm —\ndescribe the task, formalize it to the meta language, decompose, solve, and\ncompose — so that *any* repository resource can be summarized, **including\nfolders**, not only files. Rows R355-R359 capture this generalization: a\ndirectory is summarized by the recursive decompose -> summarize -> compose loop\n(`src/summarization/resource.rs`), with recursion depth bounded by the\nsummarization mode ladder.\n\n| ID | Requirement | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| R345 | The system must summarize arbitrary files from this repository, not only existing curated summarization inputs. | Implemented by `src/summarization/file.rs::{formalize_repository_file,summarize_repository_file}` and public re-exports from `src/lib.rs`; "20260703_224700_issue_626_codex_responses.md\n20260703_235500_logging_proxy.md\n20260703_issue_600_previous_user_recall.md\n20260704_000000_issue_627_agent_file_reads.md\n20260704_010000_issue_624_natural_language_ls.md\n20260704_020000_issue_628_agentic_cli_testing_guide.md\n20260704_030000_issue_526_translation_quality.md\nREADME.md\n=== sample ===\n### Added\n- Added issue #526 round-trip translation quality requirements, natural/code translation regression coverage, and case-study documentation.\n---example other---\n# Changelog Fragments\n\nThis directory contains changelog fragments that will be collected into `CHANGELOG.md` during releases.\n\n## How to Add a Changelog Fragment\n\nWhen making changes that should be documented in the changelog, create a fragment file:\n\n```bash\n# Create a new fragment with timestamp\ntouch changelog.d/$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)_description.md\n\n# Or manually create a file matching the pattern: YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_description.md\n```\n\n## Fragment Format\n\nEach fragment should include a **frontmatter section** specifying the version bump type:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n```\n\n### Bump Types\n\nUse semantic versioning bump types in the frontmatter:\n\n- **`major`**: Breaking changes (incompatible API changes)\n- **`minor`**: New features (backward compatible)\n- **`patch`**: Bug fixes (backward compatible)\n\n### Content Categories\n\nUse these categories in your fragment content:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- Description of new feature\n\n### Changed\n- Description of change to existing functionality\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n\n### Removed\n- Description of removed feature\n\n### Deprecated\n- Description of deprecated feature\n\n### Security\n- Description of security fix\n```\n\n## Examples\n\n### Adding a new feature (minor bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- New async processing mode for batch operations\n```\n\n### Fixing a bug (patch bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Fixed memory leak in connection pool handling\n```\n\n### Breaking change (major bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: major\n---\n\n### Changed\n- Renamed `process()` to `process_async()` - this is a breaking change\n\n### Removed\n- Removed deprecated `legacy_mode` option\n```\n\n## Why Fragments?\n\nUsing changelog fragments (similar to [Changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets) in JavaScript and [Scriv](https://scriv.readthedocs.io/) in Python):\n\n1. **No merge conflicts**: Multiple PRs can add fragments without conflicts\n2. **Per-PR documentation**: Each PR documents its own changes\n3. **Automated version bumping**: Version bump type is specified per-change\n4. **Automated collection**: Fragments are automatically collected during release\n5. **Consistent format**: Template ensures consistent changelog entries\n\n## How It Works\n\n1. **During PR**: Add a fragment file with your changes and bump type\n2. **On merge to main**: The release workflow automatically:\n   - Reads all fragment files and determines the highest bump type\n   - Bumps the version in `Cargo.toml` accordingly\n   - Collects fragments into `CHANGELOG.md`\n   - Creates a git tag and GitHub release\n   - Removes processed fragment files\n\n## Multiple PRs and Bump Priority\n\nWhen multiple PRs are merged before a release, all pending fragments are processed together. The **highest** bump type wins:\n\n- If any fragment specifies `major`, the release is a major version bump\n- Otherwise, if any specifies `minor`, the release is a minor version bump\n- Otherwise, the release is a patch version bump\n\nThis ensures that breaking changes are never missed, even when combined with smaller changes.\n\n## Default Behavior\n\nIf a fragment doesn't include a bump type in the frontmatter, it defaults to `patch`.",

sample ===\n### Added\n- Added issue #526 round-trip translation quality requirements, natural/code translation regression coverage, and case-study documentation.\n---example other---\n# Changelog Fragments\n\nThis directory contains changelog fragments that will be collected into `CHANGELOG.md` during releases.\n\n## How to Add a Changelog Fragment\n\nWhen making changes that should be documented in the changelog, create a fragment file:\n\n```bash\n# Create a new fragment with timestamp\ntouch changelog.d/$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)_description.md\n\n# Or manually create a file matching the pattern: YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_description.md\n```\n\n## Fragment Format\n\nEach fragment should include a **frontmatter section** specifying the version bump type:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n```\n\n### Bump Types\n\nUse semantic versioning bump types in the frontmatter:\n\n- **`major`**: Breaking changes (incompatible API changes)\n- **`minor`**: New features (backward compatible)\n- **`patch`**: Bug fixes (backward compatible)\n\n### Content Categories\n\nUse these categories in your fragment content:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- Description of new feature\n\n### Changed\n- Description of change to existing functionality\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n\n### Removed\n- Description of removed feature\n\n### Deprecated\n- Description of deprecated feature\n\n### Security\n- Description of security fix\n```\n\n## Examples\n\n### Adding a new feature (minor bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- New async processing mode for batch operations\n```\n\n### Fixing a bug (patch bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Fixed memory leak in connection pool handling\n```\n\n### Breaking change (major bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: major\n---\n\n### Changed\n- Renamed `process()` to `process_async()` - this is a breaking change\n\n### Removed\n- Removed deprecated `legacy_mode` option\n```\n\n## Why Fragments?\n\nUsing changelog fragments (similar to [Changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets) in JavaScript and [Scriv](https://scriv.readthedocs.io/) in Python):\n\n1. **No merge conflicts**: Multiple PRs can add fragments without conflicts\n2. **Per-PR documentation**: Each PR documents its own changes\n3. **Automated version bumping**: Version bump type is specified per-change\n4. **Automated collection**: Fragments are automatically collected during release\n5. **Consistent format**: Template ensures consistent changelog entries\n\n## How It Works\n\n1. **During PR**: Add a fragment file with your changes and bump type\n2. **On merge to main**: The release workflow automatically:\n   - Reads all fragment files and determines the highest bump type\n   - Bumps the version in `Cargo.toml` accordingly\n   - Collects fragments into `CHANGELOG.md`\n   - Creates a git tag and GitHub release\n   - Removes processed fragment files\n\n## Multiple PRs and Bump Priority\n\nWhen multiple PRs are merged before a release, all pending fragments are processed together. The **highest** bump type wins:\n\n- If any fragment specifies `major`, the release is a major version bump\n- Otherwise, if any specifies `minor`, the release is a minor version bump\n- Otherwise, the release is a patch version bump\n\nThis ensures that breaking changes are never missed, even when combined with smaller changes.\n\n## Default Behavior\n\nIf a fragment doesn't include a bump type in the frontmatter, it defaults to `patch`.",

      {
        "tool_use_id": "toolu_018jSU86mQ6EvnR8dmr9jHwq",
        "type": "tool_result",
        "content": "440\t/// pipeline. A fragment is first *formalized* into a `CodeMeaning` and then\n441\t/// *rendered* into the requested target, so the translator stays at `N`\n442\t/// formalizers plus `N` renderers instead of `N * N` hardcoded pairs. Adding a\n443\t/// language is one new [`formalize_code_meaning`] recognizer and one new\n444\t/// [`render_code_meaning`] arm — never a new `(source, target)` pair.\n445\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]\n446\tpub enum CodeMeaning {\n447\t    /// A binary function `add(a, b)` that returns the sum of its two\n448\t    /// parameters. This is the currently seeded code meaning; widen coverage by\n449\t    /// adding variants here rather than by adding direct language pairs.\n450\t    BinaryAddFunction,\n451\t    /// The fragment has not been formalized into a known code meaning. Carries\n452\t    /// the trimmed source so callers render a traceable gap instead of\n453\t    /// manufacturing an incorrect translation.\n454\t    Unformalized(String),\n455\t}\n456\t\n457\timpl CodeMeaning {\n458\t    /// Stable, language-neutral slug for this code meaning. Two fragments that\n459\t    /// share a meaning (e.g. the same add function written in Rust and in\n460\t    /// JavaScript) collapse to the same slug, which is what lets a round trip\n461\t    /// preserve its `meaning:` link.\n462\t    pub fn slug(&self) -> String {\n463\t        match self {\n464\t            Self::BinaryAddFunction => String::from(\"function:add:binary_sum\"),\n465\t            Self::Unformalized(source) => source\n466\t                .chars()\n467\t                .filter(char::is_ascii_alphanumeric)\n468\t                .collect::<String>()\n469\t                .to_lowercase(),\n470\t        }\n471\t    }\n472\t}\n473\t\n474\t/// Formalize a source-language code fragment into the language-neutral code\n475\t/// meta language. The source language is irrelevant to the result: `add`\n476\t/// written in Rust, Python, or JavaScript all collapse to the same\n477\t/// [`CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction`], which is exactly why the round trip\n478\t/// preserves meaning.\n479\tpub fn formalize_code_meaning(code: &str) -> CodeMeaning {\n480\t    if is_binary_add_function(code) {\n481\t        CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction\n482\t    } else {\n483\t        CodeMeaning::Unformalized(code.trim().to_owned())\n484\t    }\n485\t}\n486\t\n487\t/// Render a [`CodeMeaning`] into `target`'s surface syntax. When the meaning is\n488\t/// unknown, or the target language has no seeded rendering yet, this returns a\n489\t/// traceable, language-appropriate translation-gap comment rather than\n490\t/// inventing plausible-but-wrong code.\n491\tpub fn render_code_meaning(meaning: &CodeMeaning, source: &str, target: &str) -> String {\n492\t    if let CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction = meaning {\n493\t        if let Some(rendered) = render_binary_add(target) {\n494\t            return rendered;\n495\t        }\n496\t    }\n497\t    let subject = match meaning {\n498\t        CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction => \"add function\",\n499\t        CodeMeaning::Unformalized(source_code) => source_code.as_str(),\n500\t    };\n501\t    format!(\n502\t        \"{} translation gap for `{subject}` from {source} to {target}\",\n503\t        code_comment_prefix(target)\n504\t    )\n505\t}\n506\t\n507\t/// Render the seeded [`CodeMeaning::BinaryAddFunction`] into `target`. Returns\n508\t/// `None` when `target` has no seeded rendering, so the caller can emit a gap.\n509\tfn render_binary_add(target: &str) -> Option<String> {\n510\t    let rendered = match target {\n511\t        \"python\" => \"def add(a, b):\\n    return a + b\",\n512\t        \"rust\" => \"fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {\\n    a + b\\n}\",\n513\t        \"javascript\" => \"function add(a, b) {\\n    return a + b;\\n}\",\n514\t        \"typescript\" => \"function add(a: number, b: number): number {\\n    return a + b;\\n}\",\n515\t        \"go\" => \"func add(a int, b int) int {\\n    return a + b\\n}\",\n516\t        _ => return None,\n517\t    };\n518\t    Some(String::from(rendered))\n519\t}\n520\t\n521\t/// Comment prefix used to render a translation gap in `language`'s own syntax.\n522\tfn code_comment_prefix(language: &str) -> &'static str {\n523\t    match language {\n524\t        \"python\" | \"ruby\" => \"#\",\n525\t        _ => \"//\",\n526\t    }\n527\t}\n528\t\n529\t/// Translate a code fragment from `source` to `target` by routing through the\n530\t/// code meta language: `source -> CodeMeaning -> target`.
